title: Niṣpaṇṇayogāvali
author: Abhayākaragupta
classification_notes: Niṣpaṇṇayogāvali by Abhayākaragupta is a Buddhist tantric text describing maṇḍala rituals. It is an important work within Vajrayāna studies, though specialized.

title: Niṣpaṇṇayogāvali
author: Abhayākaragupta
classification_notes: Abhayākaragupta’s Niṣpaṇṇayogāvali (‘Garland of Accomplished Yoga’) is a concise Buddhist tantric manual describing the iconometric details and visualization practices of several meditation deities. Its brevity (about 700 words) and technical nature confine its readership largely to specialists in Vajrayāna iconography and literature.

title: Kādambarīkathāsāra
author: Abhinanda
classification_notes: The Kādambarīkathāsāra by Abhinanda is a poetic abridgement of Bāṇa's famous prose romance Kādambarī. It condenses the elaborate original into a more accessible narrative verse form. While the original Kādambarī is a major kāvya work, Abhinanda's version is of interest mainly to specialists in Sanskrit narrative and literary adaptation.

title: Kādambarīkathāsāra
author: Abhinanda
classification_notes: Abhinanda’s Kādambarīkathāsāra (12,787 words) is a poetic abridgment of Bāṇa’s great prose romance Kādambarī. It preserves the narrative core in an elegant kathā form and is a commonly referenced work in Sanskrit literary studies.

title: Rāmacaritamahākāvya
author: Abhinanda
classification_notes: The Rāmacaritamahākāvya by Abhinanda is a Sanskrit court epic (mahākāvya) retelling the Rāma story in ornate verse. It is a well-known but not canonical work within the kāvya tradition, read by specialists in Sanskrit poetry and Rāma literature.

title: Rāmacaritamahākāvya
author: Abhinanda
classification_notes: Abhinanda's Rāmacaritamahākāvya is a Sanskrit courtly epic retelling the story of Rāma in ornate kāvya style. While the Rāma narrative is universally known, this particular mahākāvya (ca. 5100 words) is not as widely read as the Rāmāyaṇa of Vālmīki or the works of Kālidāsa, positioning it as a specialist work within the vast corpus of Sanskrit literary epic.

title: Anuttarāṣṭikā
author: Abhinavagupta
classification_notes: Abhinavagupta's Anuttarāṣṭikā is a brief but philosophically dense stotra encapsulating the essence of the Pratyabhijñā system of Kashmir Śaivism. It is a specialist text valued within Tantric and Śaiva philosophical circles.

title: Anuttarāṣṭikā
author: Abhinavagupta
classification_notes: Abhinavagupta's Anuttarāṣṭikā is a short hymn (stotra) of eight verses devoted to the supreme nondual reality (Anuttara) in the Trika Śaiva tradition. Composed by the master philosopher and mystic, this dense, mantra-like poem encapsulates the essence of his nondual Tantric theology. Within the Śaiva tantric scope it holds common appeal as a praised example of stotra literature. However, across the global Sanskrit corpus, its extreme brevity (only 235 words) and esoteric doctrinal content make it a specialist text.

title: Ghaṭakarparavivṛti
author: Abhinavagupta
classification_notes: The Ghaṭakarparavivṛti by Abhinavagupta is a 3,655-word commentary on the Ghaṭakarpara, a Prakrit/Sanskrit lyric poem. Abhinavagupta, a leading Śaiva philosopher, applies his poetic theory (dhvani) to this kāvya. The work is specialist globally but common within Sanskrit poetics and kāvya commentary.

title: Ghaṭakarparavivṛti
author: Abhinavagupta
classification_notes: The Ghaṭakarparavivṛti is a commentary by the Kashmir Śaiva polymath Abhinavagupta on the Ghaṭakarpara Kāvya, a short Sanskrit poem. Its 3,493 words provide a philosophical and aesthetic reading of the verse. Important within Kāvya and Śaiva commentarial traditions, but specialist in the wider Sanskrit canon.

title: Kramastotra
author: Abhinavagupta
classification_notes: The Kramastotra by Abhinavagupta is a devotional hymn of the Kashmir Śaiva tradition, composed by the polymath philosopher and mystic Abhinavagupta. The stotra is a liturgical and meditative text praising the goddess (Kālī) of the Krama system. It is a well-regarded piece within tantric devotional literature, studied for its theological and poetic dimensions.

title: Kramastotra
author: Abhinavagupta
classification_notes: The Kramastotra is a short devotional hymn (stotra) composed by Abhinavagupta, the great Kashmiri Śaiva philosopher and aesthetician. With only 682 tokens, it is a compact but theologically rich praise poem dedicated to the goddess (Devi) in the Krama tradition of Kashmir Śaivism. The work holds specialist status in the broad Sanskrit corpus, but within the genre of Śaiva stotra literature it is a common reference, valued for its integration of Tantric philosophy with lyrical devotion.

title: Mālinīślokavārttika
author: Abhinavagupta
classification_notes: Abhinavagupta's Mālinīślokavārttika is a philosophical commentary on the Mālinīvijayottara Tantra, a key text of the Kashmir Śaiva tradition. It is highly regarded within Tantric and Śaiva studies but remains a specialist text in the broader Sanskrit corpus.

title: Mālinīślokavārttika
author: Abhinavagupta
classification_notes: The Mālinīślokavārttika is a philosophical commentary by Abhinavagupta on the Mālinīvijayottaratantra, a key text of Kashmir Śaivism. It is a specialist work in the wider Sanskrit context but common within Śaiva Tantra studies.

title: Paramārthasāra
author: Abhinavagupta
classification_notes: Abhinavagupta's Paramārthasāra is a concise exposition of Śaiva non-dualism. It is a key text in the Pratyabhijñā school and is widely studied in tantric and philosophical contexts.

title: Paramārthasāra
author: Abhinavagupta
classification_notes: Abhinavagupta's Paramārthasāra is a concise exposition of non-dual Kashmiri Śaiva philosophy. It distills key doctrines of the Trika system and is valued by scholars of Śaiva theology and Indian philosophy.

title: Paryantapañcāśikā
author: Abhinavagupta
classification_notes: The Paryantapañcāśikā by the Kashmiri Śaiva philosopher Abhinavagupta is a 790-word hymn (stotra) of fifty verses celebrating the supreme reality as the union of Śiva and Śakti. It is a devotional and theological work deeply embedded in the Pratyabhijñā school. The text is significant within Śaiva Tantra and is studied by specialists in Kashmiri Śaivism and Tantric literature.

title: Paryantapañcāśikā
author: Abhinavagupta
classification_notes: Abhinavagupta's Paryantapañcāśikā is a short, fifty-verse hymn (stotra) of the Śaiva Tantric tradition. Composed by the preeminent philosopher and mystic Abhinavagupta, this work is a devotional and meditative text. Its brevity (687 words) and highly specialized theological content make it a specialist text, primarily of interest to scholars of Kashmir Śaivism and Tantric literature. Within the religion discovery group, it is a minor but valued work for specialists.

title: Parātriṃśikāvivaraṇa
author: Abhinavagupta
classification_notes: Abhinavagupta's Parātriṃśikāvivaraṇa is a detailed commentary on the Parātriṃśikā, a core text of Kashmiri Śaiva Tantra. It is essential for advanced study of Śaiva exegesis and Tantric philosophy.

title: Parātriṃśikāvivaraṇa
author: Abhinavagupta
classification_notes: Abhinavagupta's Parātriṃśikāvivaraṇa is a poignant philosophical commentary on the Parātriṃśikā, a core scripture of the Trika Śaiva tradition. Written by the towering figure of Kashmiri Śaivism, this vivaraṇaprakāśa (expository commentary) elaborates the mantric and metaphysical dimensions of the goddess Parā's thirty syllables, integrating Tantra, aesthetics, and nondual philosophy. It holds a central place within the Śaiva tantric exegetical corpus but commands specialist rather than general interest in the broader Sanskrit corpus, given its dense technicality and tradition-specific doctrinal apparatus.

title: Tantrāloka
author: Abhinavagupta
classification_notes: Abhinavagupta's Tantrāloka is a magnum opus of the Kashmir Śaiva tradition, a comprehensive and sophisticated exposition of Śaiva Tantric philosophy, ritual, and theology. It is a foundational text for the Pratyabhijñā school and is widely studied in the fields of Tantric studies and Indian philosophy. Its length (22218 words) represents a substantial portion of this extensive work.

title: Advayaśatikā prajñāpāramitā
author: Advayaśatikā
classification_notes: The Advayaśatikā prajñāpāramitā is a short Buddhist sūtra belonging to the Prajñāpāramitā (Perfection of Wisdom) genre. Attributed to Advayaśatikā, this text presents teachings on non-duality (advaya) and emptiness (śūnyatā). It is a concise but theologically significant work within the Mahāyāna Buddhist tradition, of interest to specialists in Buddhist scripture and philosophy.

title: Lāṭyāyanaśrautasūtrabhāṣya
author: Agnisvāmin
classification_notes: Agnisvāmin's Lāṭyāyanaśrautasūtrabhāṣya is a medieval commentary on the Lāṭyāyanaśrautasūtra, a ritual manual of the Sāmavedic tradition. As a bhāṣya, it provides explanations of the sūtras and elaborates on the ritual procedures. This work is of specialized interest for scholars of Vedic ritual and the medieval commentarial tradition, preserving access to the interpretation of Sāmavedic śrauta rites.

title: Lāṭyāyanaśrautasūtrabhāṣya
author: Agnisvāmin
classification_notes: The Lāṭyāyanaśrautasūtrabhāṣya by Agnisvāmin (c. 11th–12th century CE) is a 27,697-word commentary on the Lāṭyāyanaśrautasūtra. It explains the Sāmavedic ritual procedures in the brāhmaṇa style. The text is specialist globally and specialist within Vedic ritual exegesis.

title: Nyāyasūtra
author: Akṣapāda Gautama
classification_notes: The Nyāyasūtra, attributed to Akṣapāda Gautama, is the foundational text of the Nyāya school of Hindu philosophy, presenting a system of logic, epistemology, and debate. It is widely studied across the Sanskrit philosophical tradition and constitutes a core source for Indological and philosophical scholarship.

title: Amarakośa
author: Amarasiṃha
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Lexicography; dcs_subject=Kosha; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Amara; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Amarasiṃha's Amarakośa is the most celebrated thesaurus in Sanskrit literature. A foundational text for Sanskrit lexicography, it has been used as a standard reference for over a millennium. Its wide and continuous usage among students and scholars of Sanskrit grants it a common global popularity and a major status within its field.

title: Amaruśataka
author: Amaru
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Kavya; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Amaru; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Amaruśataka is a celebrated Sanskrit lyric collection of one hundred stanzas attributed to the poet Amaru. It explores love and erotic sentiment (śṛṅgāra rasa) through concise, vivid vignettes. The work holds a common position in the broader Sanskrit corpus and is a major example within the kavya (ornate poetry) tradition, frequently anthologized and commented upon.

title: Tarkasaṃgraha
author: Annambhaṭṭa
classification_notes: Annambhaṭṭa's Tarkasaṃgraha is a concise yet comprehensive introduction to Nyaya philosophy, widely used as a foundational textbook in Indian logic. It systematically presents categories, inference, and epistemology, and remains a standard reference for students of Indian philosophy.

title: Ṣaḍviṃśa Brāhmaṇa
author: Anonymous
classification_notes: The Ṣaḍviṃśa Brāhmaṇa is a Brāhmaṇa text belonging to the Sāmaveda, specifically the Kauthuma school. It is a late Brāhmaṇa, supplementing the Pañcaviṃśa Brāhmaṇa, and contains explanations of Sāmavedic rituals and mythology. It is a significant text for the study of Vedic ritual and Sāmavedic exegesis, of common interest within the specialist field of Vedic studies. Its length (8045 words) is moderate for a Brāhmaṇa.

title: Ṣaḍviṃśa Brāhmaṇa
author: Anonymous
classification_notes: The Ṣaḍviṃśa Brāhmaṇa is a minor Brāhmaṇa of the Sāmaveda, supplementing the larger Pañcaviṃśa Brāhmaṇa. It provides additional ritual instructions and mythological explanations for Sāmavedic liturgical practice. Of primary interest to specialists in Vedic literature and ritual, its restricted scope and esoteric content keep its readership within a narrow scholarly circle.

title: Vairāgyaśatakam
author: Appayadīkṣita
classification_notes: Vairāgyaśatakam ('Century of Dispassion') is a philosophical poem by the renowned polymath Appayadīkṣita. It urges renunciation and reflects the Advaita Vedanta perspective. The work showcases Appayadīkṣita's poetic skill and is valued in the kāvya tradition.

title: Vairāgyaśatakam
author: Appayadīkṣita
classification_notes: Appayadīkṣita's Vairāgyaśatakam is a collection of a hundred verses on dispassion and renunciation, composed in the classical kāvya style by one of the most influential polymaths of the late sixteenth century. Though overshadowed by his massive philosophical and exegetical output, this short poetic work exemplifies the intersection of bhakti devotion and Advaita Vedānta in the vernacularizing Early Modern period. Its specialist readership reflects the niche appeal of post-Śaṅkara doxographic poetry composed by a scholar primarily known for technical treatises.

title: Abhidharmasamuccaya
author: Asaṅga
classification_notes: Asaṅga's Abhidharmasamuccaya is a foundational Yogācāra Buddhist philosophical text that systematically presents Abhidharma categories from a Mahāyāna perspective. Composed in the 4th–5th century CE, it is a key work for the study of Buddhist philosophy, particularly the Yogācāra school, and is widely cited in scholarly literature on Indian Buddhist thought.

title: Abhidharmasamuccaya
author: Asaṅga
classification_notes: The Abhidharmasamuccaya by Asaṅga (ca. 17,679 tokens) is a comprehensive compendium of Abhidharma philosophy from the Yogācāra perspective. It is a major text in Buddhist scholasticism, widely studied in Buddhist studies programs.

title: Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra
author: Asaṅga
classification_notes: The Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra (Ornament of the Mahāyāna Sūtras) is a key Yogācāra treatise attributed to Asaṅga. It expounds the Mahāyāna path, the bodhisattva ideal, and the nature of emptiness. It is a central text for the study of Yogācāra philosophy and is commonly taught in graduate courses on Buddhist thought.

title: Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra
author: Asaṅga
classification_notes: Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra is a seminal Mahayana Buddhist text attributed to Asaṅga, presenting the bodhisattva path and Yogācāra philosophy. It is widely studied in Buddhist scholastic traditions.

title: Triśatikāyāḥ prajñāpāramitāyāḥ kārikāsaptatiḥ
author: Asaṅga
classification_notes: The Triśatikāyāḥ Prajñāpāramitāyāḥ Kārikāsaptatiḥ by Asaṅga is a 1,064-word verse summary in 70 kārikās of the Triśatikā Prajñāpāramitā sūtra. As part of the Yogācāra tradition, it condenses Prajñāpāramitā philosophy. The work is specialist in the Sanskrit corpus and specialist within Buddhist scripture.

title: Triśatikāyāḥ prajñāpāramitāyāḥ kārikāsaptatiḥ
author: Asaṅga
classification_notes: Asaṅga's Triśatikāyāḥ Prajñāpāramitāyāḥ Kārikāsaptatiḥ (Seventy Verses on the Three Hundred Perfections of Wisdom) is a short Yogacara verse text summarizing the Prajnaparamita. Its specialized content, of interest to Buddhist philosophical scholars, restricts it to a specialist audience.

title: Śarīrārthagāthā
author: Asaṅga
classification_notes: The Śarīrārthagāthā by Asaṅga is a Buddhist philosophical work, likely a verse summary of key doctrines from the Yogācāra school. Asaṅga is a foundational figure in Mahāyāna Buddhist philosophy, and this text, though brief, contributes to the understanding of his thought on the nature of reality and consciousness. It is of interest to specialists in Buddhist philosophy and the Yogācāra tradition.

title: Śarīrārthagāthā
author: Asaṅga
classification_notes: Śarīrārthagāthā is a short Buddhist philosophical work attributed to Asaṅga, a founder of the Yogācāra school. It deals with the nature of the body and reality.

title: Śrāvakabhūmi
author: Asaṅga
classification_notes: Asaṅga’s Śrāvakabhūmi is a key text of the Yogācāra school, detailing the stages of the spiritual path for hearers (śrāvaka). It is an essential source for the study of early Buddhist scholasticism and Mahāyāna philosophy.

title: Śrāvakabhūmi
author: Asaṅga
classification_notes: Asaṅga's Śrāvakabhūmi is a key Yogācāra text, forming part of the larger Yogācārabhūmi Śāstra. It systematically outlines the stages and practices of the śrāvaka (disciple) path towards liberation. It is a foundational work for the study of Yogācāra Buddhist philosophy and meditation theory. Its length (42593 words) is substantial, indicating a comprehensive treatment of the subject.

title: Bhagavadbhaktistotra
author: Avadhūtasiddha
classification_notes: The Bhagavadbhaktistotra by Avadhūtasiddha is a 1540-word Sanskrit hymn praising devotion to Lord Viṣṇu. Composed in the early medieval period, this stotra blends bhakti sentiment with philosophical elements from the Avadhūta tradition. While central to the devotional corpus of its school, it remains a specialist text outside the broader Sanskrit hymn tradition.

title: Bhagavadbhaktistotra
author: Avadhūtasiddha
classification_notes: Bhagavadbhaktistotra is a Sanskrit hymn of devotion to Krishna, attributed to Avadhūtasiddha. It belongs to the Stotra tradition and is valued in certain bhakti circles.

title: Buddhacarita
author: Aśvaghoṣa
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Buddhist Scripture; dcs_subject=Buddhist; dcs_time_slot=epic; dcs_author=Aśvaghoṣa; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Aśvaghoṣa's Buddhacarita is a Sanskrit epic poem narrating the life of the Buddha. It is a masterpiece of Buddhist literature and a key text for the study of classical Sanskrit poetry and Buddhist hagiography.

title: Gurupañcāśikā
author: Aśvaghoṣa
classification_notes: Aśvaghoṣa's Gurupañcāśikā is a short Buddhist stotra (hymn) in Sanskrit, praised by the poet of the Buddhacarita. Comprising 50 verses, it venerates the teacher (guru) and is a minor work within the kāvya tradition. It is of interest mainly to specialists in Buddhist Sanskrit poetry.

title: Gurupañcāśikā
author: Aśvaghoṣa
classification_notes: Gurupañcāśikā is a short devotional hymn in Sanskrit by the renowned Buddhist poet Aśvaghoṣa, consisting of fifty verses extolling the spiritual teacher. It reflects the early tradition of guru veneration in Buddhism and is an example of stotra literature.

title: Saundarānanda
author: Aśvaghoṣa
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Kavya; dcs_time_slot=epic; dcs_author=Aśvaghoṣa; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Saundarānanda (The Beauty of Nanda) is a celebrated mahākāvya by the great Buddhist poet Aśvaghoṣa, composed in the early centuries CE. With 12,122 words, it tells the story of the Buddha's half-brother Nanda's path from worldly attachment to monastic renunciation, blending high literary style with Buddhist doctrinal exposition. It is a masterpiece of Buddhist literature and a key text for the study of early Indian kāvya.

title: Vajrasūcī
author: Aśvaghoṣa
classification_notes: A polemical text attributed to Aśvaghoṣa, the Vajrasūcī ('Diamond Needle') is a work that argues against caste distinctions from a Buddhist perspective. It is a relatively short prose work of 787 words. Aśvaghoṣa's authorship is sometimes questioned, but the text remains of interest for the study of Buddhist social thought and Sanskrit religious dialogue.

title: Vajrasūcī
author: Aśvaghoṣa
classification_notes: Vajrasūcī is a short Buddhist polemical text attributed to Aśvaghoṣa (though disputed), critically examining caste hierarchies and advocating spiritual equality. It is of specialist interest in the wider Sanskrit corpus and common within Buddhist scripture studies.

title: Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā
author: Aṣṭasāhasrikā
classification_notes: The Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā is a foundational Mahāyāna Buddhist sūtra representing the earliest Perfection of Wisdom literature. It is widely studied in both textual and doctrinal contexts, central to Buddhist philosophy and practice. Its importance in the Buddhist tradition makes it a major work within the whole Sanskrit corpus.

title: Bhallaṭaśataka
author: Bhallaṭa
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Kavya; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Bhallaṭa; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Bhallaṭaśataka is a sataka (century of verses) attributed to Bhallaṭa, a lesser-known classical Sanskrit poet. The work survives as an anthology of single-stanza poems on varied topics—amorous sentiment, nature description, and ethical reflection. Its specialist readership within the kāvya tradition stems from its role as a representative of the subgenre of detached verse collections (kośa, sataka) that preserve the flavor of early medieval courtly poetics. The work's moderate word count and fragmentary transmission history place it among texts of interest primarily to specialists of classical lyric poetry.

title: Nāṭyaśāstra
author: Bharata Muni
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Drama; dcs_subject=Natyashastra; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Bharata; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Nāṭyaśāstra, attributed to the sage Bharata Muni, is the foundational treatise on Sanskrit dramaturgy and performance. It is a comprehensive encyclopedic work on theater, dance, music, and aesthetics, containing the famous rasa theory. This text is central to the study of Indian aesthetics and is widely cited across disciplines including literary theory, theater history, and musicology.

title: Śatakatraya
author: Bhartṛhari
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Kavya; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Bhartṛhari; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Śatakatraya ('Three Centuries') of Bhartṛhari is one of the most celebrated collections of Sanskrit lyric poetry. Comprising three centuries of verses on ethics (nīti), love (śṛṅgāra), and renunciation (vairāgya), it is a masterpiece of classical kāvya literature. Its aphoristic style, philosophical depth, and literary elegance have made it a widely studied and beloved text across the Sanskrit tradition.

title: Jaiminīyaśrautasūtrabhāṣya
author: Bhavatrāta
classification_notes: Bhavatrāta's Jaiminīyaśrautasūtrabhāṣya is a commentary on the Jaiminīya Śrautasūtra, crucial for understanding the Sāmavedic ritual tradition. Its length and detail make it valuable for specialists in Vedic ritual, but its highly technical content and narrow audience place it in the specialist tier globally.

title: Jaiminīyaśrautasūtrabhāṣya
author: Bhavatrāta
classification_notes: Bhavatrāta's Jaiminīyaśrautasūtrabhāṣya is a commentary on the Jaiminīya Śrauta Sūtra of the Sāmaveda, explaining the procedures of large-scale Vedic sacrifices. It is a specialized scholarly work that sheds light on the performance of Vedic ritual as interpreted in the commentarial tradition.

title: Rāvaṇavadha
author: Bhaṭṭi
classification_notes: The Rāvaṇavadha ('Slaying of Rāvaṇa') by Bhaṭṭi, also known as the Bhaṭṭikāvya, is a celebrated Sanskrit poem that retells the story of the Rāmāyaṇa while simultaneously illustrating the rules of Sanskrit grammar and poetics. It is a unique blend of epic narrative and grammatical instruction, making it a key text for the study of classical kāvya and the Sanskrit grammatical tradition.

title: Rāvaṇavadha
author: Bhaṭṭi
classification_notes: The Rāvaṇavadha by Bhaṭṭi is a classical Sanskrit kavya that retells the Ramayana story while also serving as a pedagogical illustration of Sanskrit grammar and figures of speech. It is a commonly studied work, known for its dual didactic and literary purpose.

title: Rājamārtaṇḍa
author: Bhoja
classification_notes: Bhoja's Rājamārtaṇḍa is a commentary on Patañjali's Yogasūtras. It is one of the earliest and most influential commentaries, elucidating Yoga philosophy and practice. It remains a key text for Yoga studies.

title: Rājamārtaṇḍa
author: Bhoja
classification_notes: Bhoja's Rājamārtaṇḍa is a celebrated commentary on Patañjali's Yogasūtras. Named after its royal author, the commentary (ca. 13700 words) presents a theistic interpretation of the Yoga system, integrating Vedāntic themes. It is widely consulted by students and scholars of Yoga philosophy, giving it a solid presence within the specialist community and beyond into comparative religious studies.

title: Kirātārjunīya
author: Bhāravi
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Kavya; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Bhāravi; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Bhāravi's Kirātārjunīya is a landmark of Sanskrit mahākāvya (great poem), recounting the episode of Arjuna's penance and his encounter with Śiva in the guise of a kirāta (hunter). It is celebrated for its linguistic virtuosity, intricate structure, and philosophical depth. Along with Kālidāsa's works, it is one of the most widely read and studied examples of the classical Sanskrit epic-lyric tradition, achieving a 'common' popularity tier due to its place in curricula and anthologies.

title: Dūtavākya
author: Bhāsa
classification_notes: Bhāsa's Dūtavākya is a short one-act Sanskrit play based on a pivotal episode from the Mahābhārata: the peace mission of Kṛṣṇa to the Kaurava court before the Kuruksetra war. Bhāsa, a classical playwright predating Kālidāsa, is known for his dramatic economy and powerful character portrayal. This play forms part of the 'Trivandrum Plays' attributed to Bhāsa. Within the realm of Sanskrit drama, it is a work of common importance; across the entire Sanskrit corpus, it remains specialist, valued by students and scholars of classical Sanskrit theater and epic narrative adaptation.

title: Dūtavākya
author: Bhāsa
classification_notes: Bhāsa's Dūtavākya (The Messenger's Speech) is a one-act Sanskrit play based on the Mahabharata. Bhāsa is a major dramatist, and his works are important for the study of Sanskrit drama. This play is well-regarded within the field, but is less widely read than works by Kālidāsa, placing it at the specialist end of global popularity.

title: Mokṣopāyaṭīkā
author: Bhāskarakaṇṭha
classification_notes: The Mokṣopāyaṭīkā by Bhāskarakaṇṭha is a substantial commentary (ṭīkā) on the Mokṣopāya, a philosophical text in the Advaita Vedānta tradition. With 90899 words, this medieval work provides detailed exposition and analysis of the original text's arguments on liberation and non-dualism. It is a specialist resource for scholars of Kashmiri Śaivism and Advaita Vedānta.

title: Mokṣopāyaṭīkā
author: Bhāskarakaṇṭha
classification_notes: The Mokṣopāyaṭīkā, authored by Bhāskarakaṇṭha, is a commentary on the Mokṣopāya (or Yogavāsiṣṭha), a vast philosophical work blending Advaita Vedānta and Yoga. Bhāskarakaṇṭha's commentary is a significant resource for interpreting this complex text, which explores the nature of reality, consciousness, and liberation. Despite the depth of the parent work, the commentary itself is a specialist tool for scholars of Advaita and Kashmiri non-dualism.

title: Bhāvaprakāśa
author: Bhāvamiśra
classification_notes: Bhāvamiśra's Bhāvaprakāśa is one of the most influential medieval-to-early-modern compendia of Ayurvedic medicine, organized into sections on materia medica, pathology, diagnosis, and therapeutics. It integrates herbal lore from earlier nighaṇṭus with systematic treatment protocols. While the work holds common popularity within the Ayurveda scope and is frequently referenced by practitioners and scholars of traditional medicine, its global prominence within the Sanskrit corpus is specialist, as its technical medical content is of circumscribed interest outside Indological medicine history.

title: Tattvayāthārthyadīpana
author: Bhāvāgaṇeśa
classification_notes: The Tattvayāthārthyadīpana by Bhāvāgaṇeśa is a 6310-word medieval Sanskrit philosophical treatise that examines the true nature of reality (tattva) through a critical lens, blending Advaita Vedānta and Nyāya logic. This work is a specialist contribution to the scholastic literature of Indian philosophy, of interest primarily to historians of later Vedantic and logical traditions.

title: Tattvayāthārthyadīpana
author: Bhāvāgaṇeśa
classification_notes: The Tattvayāthārthyadīpana by Bhāvāgaṇeśa is a specialist work in Nyaya philosophy, likely a commentary or exposition on truth and reality. It is important for researchers of post-classical Nyaya.

title: Caurapañcaśikā
author: Bilhaṇa
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Kavya; dcs_time_slot=late; dcs_author=Bilhaṇa; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Caurapañcaśikā by Bilhaṇa is a celebrated Sanskrit lyric poem of fifty stanzas on the theme of illicit love. A specialist work in the broader corpus, it holds a common position within Sanskrit kavya.

title: Brahmapurāṇa
author: Brahmapurāṇa
classification_notes: The Brahmapurāṇa is one of the eighteen principal Mahāpurāṇas, a vast compendium (ca. 202000 words) of mythology, cosmology, genealogy, and religious instruction. While not as widely read as the Bhāgavata or Viṣṇu Purāṇa, it holds canonical status within the purāṇic corpus and is frequently cited in scholarly works on Hindu mythology and medieval sectarianism.

title: Siddhārthacaritrakāvya
author: Buddhaghoṣa
classification_notes: The Siddhārthacaritrakāvya by Buddhaghoṣa is a Sanskrit kāvya recounting the life of the Buddha Śākyamuni from his renunciation to enlightenment. This 11695-word poetic biography blends Buddhist doctrine with the conventions of classical Indian courtly poetry (kāvya). It is a notable work in the Buddhist literary tradition and is read by specialists in Buddhist studies and Sanskrit literature.

title: Siddhārthacaritrakāvya
author: Buddhaghoṣa
classification_notes: Buddhaghoṣa's Siddhārthacaritrakāvya is a Buddhist kāvya poem narrating the life of the Buddha (Siddhārtha). Composed by the renowned Buddhist commentator Buddhaghoṣa, this work (7,494 words) is a significant example of Buddhist literary poetry in Sanskrit. While Buddhaghoṣa is a major figure in Buddhist literature, this particular poem is less widely studied than his Pali commentaries, placing it in the specialist tier globally and within the poetry discovery group.

title: Bṛhatkathāślokasaṃgraha
author: Budhasvāmin
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Narrative Literature; dcs_subject=Katha; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Buddhasvāmin; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Bṛhatkathāślokasaṃgraha is a Sanskrit verse epitome of the lost Bṛhatkathā, attributed to Budhasvāmin. It is a vital source for the study of Sanskrit narrative literature and the development of the katha genre.

title: Brahmasūtra
author: Bādarāyaṇa
classification_notes: The Brahmasūtra by Bādarāyaṇa is the foundational text of the Vedānta school of Indian philosophy, systematizing the teachings of the Upaniṣads. It is among the most studied and commented-upon works in the Sanskrit philosophical tradition, with numerous commentaries by major thinkers such as Śaṅkara and Rāmānuja. Its 555 aphorisms are essential reading for any study of Indian philosophy.

title: Brahmasūtra
author: Bādarāyaṇa
classification_notes: Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtra is the foundational text of the Vedānta tradition, systematizing the Upaniṣads in 555 aphorisms. It sets forth the nature of Brahman, the soul, and the path to liberation, and has generated an extensive commentarial tradition. It is one of the three primary sources (prasthānatrayī) of Vedānta philosophy and is absolutely canonical in Indian thought.

title: Harṣacaritapariśiṣṭa
author: Bāṇa
classification_notes: The Harṣacaritapariśiṣṭa by Bāṇa is a brief supplementary narrative (parishishta) appended to the Harshacharita. Though from a major Classical author, this short work of 314 words is a minor appendix piece. It is primarily of interest to scholars of Bāṇa's oeuvre and Classical Sanskrit narrative prose, giving it specialist popularity overall.

title: Harṣacaritapariśiṣṭa
author: Bāṇa
classification_notes: Bāṇa's Harṣacaritapariśiṣṭa is a supplementary appendix to his celebrated Harṣacarita, possibly providing additional episodes or clarifications. At only ca. 280 words, it is a brief fragment, but its association with one of the greatest Sanskrit prose writers ensures scholarly attention from specialists in classical Sanskrit narrative and courtly biography.

title: Harṣacarita
author: Bāṇabhaṭṭa
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Narrative Literature; dcs_subject=Katha; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Bāṇa; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Bāṇabhaṭṭa's Harṣacarita is a celebrated prose biography of King Harṣavardhana, blending historical events with literary artistry. It is a masterpiece of Sanskrit kāvya prose, offering rich descriptions and a unique insight into early medieval Indian court life.

title: Śiṣyalekha
author: Candragomin
classification_notes: The Śiṣyalekha ('Letter to a Disciple') by Candragomin is a celebrated Buddhist epistolary poem in Sanskrit, offering moral and spiritual advice in elegant kāvya style. It is a well-known work within Buddhist Sanskrit literature, admired for its literary qualities and its blend of Buddhist ethics with classical poetic form. Its moderate length (c. 2,769 words) is typical of a didactic poem. While respected, its readership is largely confined to specialists in Buddhist kāvya.

title: Śiṣyalekha
author: Candragomin
classification_notes: The Śiṣyalekha by Candragomin is a Buddhist epistolary poem in Sanskrit (c. 5th–7th century CE), presenting ethical and spiritual advice to a disciple. Its 1,892 words of elegant verse combine lay and monastic guidance with an early tantric flavor. The work is specialist in the Sanskrit corpus and specialist within Buddhist scripture.

title: Prasannapadā
author: Candrakīrti
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Buddhist Scripture; dcs_subject=Buddhist; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Candrakīrti; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Candrakīrti's Prasannapadā is a landmark commentary on Nāgārjuna's Mūlamadhyamakakārikā, the foundational text of the Madhyamaka school of Buddhist philosophy. Although the DCS entry records only 186 words (likely a fragment or excerpt), the work as a whole is a classic of Buddhist logic and dialectics. It is widely studied by scholars of Buddhist and Indian philosophy.

title: Carakasaṃhitā
author: Caraka
classification_notes: The Carakasaṃhitā is the foundational text of Ayurvedic internal medicine, traditionally attributed to Caraka. It remains a living source of medical knowledge and is universally studied in Ayurveda.

title: Daśakumāracarita
author: Daṇḍin
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Narrative Literature; dcs_subject=Katha; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Daṇḍin; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Daṇḍin's Daśakumāracarita ('Tales of the Ten Princes') is a celebrated work of Sanskrit prose narrative (kathā) blending romance, adventure, and social observation. As a classic of Sanskrit kāvya, it is commonly read in university curricula and anthologies, achieving a 'common' popularity tier within the broader Sanskrit corpus. Its length (c. 19,300 words) is substantial for a single work of narrative prose, and its vivid storytelling has ensured its enduring appeal.

title: Kāvyādarśa
author: Daṇḍin
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Alamkarashastra; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Daṇḍin; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Daṇḍin's Kāvyādarśa is one of the foundational treatises of Sanskrit poetics (alaṃkāraśāstra). It systematically analyses figures of speech, poetic qualities, and compositional ideals. As a core text in the tradition of literary theory, it is widely studied across Sanskrit curricula and has shaped both Indian aesthetic theory and comparative literary criticism.

title: Kāṭhagṛhyasūtrabhāṣya
author: Devapāla
classification_notes: A commentary (bhāṣya) on the Kāṭhagṛhyasūtra, a domestic ritual manual of the Kāṭhaka school of the Yajurveda. Devapāla's work explains the sūtras in detail, drawing on earlier ritual exegesis. It is a significant text for specialists in the study of Vedic ritual and domestic life, with a readership primarily among Indological scholars.

title: Kāṭhagṛhyasūtrabhāṣya
author: Devapāla
classification_notes: The Kāṭhagṛhyasūtrabhāṣya by Devapāla is a commentary on the domestic ritual sūtra of the Kāṭhaka school of the Yajurveda. It is an obscure text within the Vedic commentarial tradition, of interest primarily to specialists in Gṛhya ritual philology.

title: Drāhyāyaṇaśrautasūtrabhāṣya
author: Dhanvin
classification_notes: Dhanvin's commentary on the Drāhyāyaṇaśrautasūtra is a technical scholastic work within Vedic ritual exegesis. Although its author is known primarily from this commentary, the text is important for specialists in Śrauta rituals and the Drāhyāyaṇa school. Its modest length and focused scope place it among specialist literature within the wider Sanskrit corpus, though it commands respect within the ritual commentary tradition.

title: Drāhyāyaṇaśrautasūtrabhāṣya
author: Dhanvin
classification_notes: Dhanvin's Drāhyāyaṇaśrautasūtrabhāṣya is a medieval commentary on the Drāhyāyaṇaśrautasūtra, a ritual manual of the Sāmavedic tradition. The commentary explains the ritual procedures and sūtras of the somayāga and other sacrifices. It is of interest to specialists in Vedic ritual and the commentarial traditions of the Sāmaveda.

title: Hetubindu
author: Dharmakīrti
classification_notes: Classification: The 'Hetubindu' by Dharmakīrti is a central work of Buddhist logic and epistemology, providing a concise treatise on the theory of reasons. As a core philosophical text of the Buddhist pramāṇa tradition, it is common overall globally and major within the Buddhist philosophical group.

title: Hetubindu
author: Dharmakīrti
classification_notes: The Hetubindu, by the illustrious Buddhist logician and epistemologist Dharmakīrti, is a seminal work in Buddhist logic and epistemology. It focuses on the theory of inference (hetu) and the nature of reasons in argumentation. As a key text in the Buddhist pramāṇa tradition, it is widely studied by specialists in Buddhist philosophy and Indian logic, but its technical nature places it outside the mainstream of general Sanskrit readership.

title: Vādanyāya
author: Dharmakīrti
classification_notes: The Vādanyāya (Logic of Debate) by the Buddhist logician Dharmakīrti is a seminal work on argumentation, inference, and epistemological proof within the Buddhist logical tradition. This 10,326-word text is a key component of the pramāṇa corpus, extending Dharmakīrti's earlier work in the Pramāṇavārttika. It is widely studied in Buddhist and Indian philosophical circles, particularly within Tibetan scholasticism.

title: Vādanyāya
author: Dharmakīrti
classification_notes: Vādanyāya is a major work of Buddhist logic and epistemology by Dharmakīrti, part of his series of texts on pramana theory. It is widely studied in Indian and Tibetan Buddhist philosophical traditions and is a cornerstone of the Buddhist logical tradition.

title: Gauragovindārcanasmaraṇapaddhati
author: Dhyānacandragosvāmin
classification_notes: The Gauragovindārcanasmaraṇapaddhati by Dhyānacandragosvāmin is an early modern Vaiṣṇava ritual manual dedicated to the worship and remembrance of Caitanya (as Gauragovinda). It is an obscure text restricted to specialists in Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava ritual.

title: Gauragovindārcanasmaraṇapaddhati
author: Dhyānacandragosvāmin
classification_notes: Dhyānacandragosvāmin's Gauragovindārcanasmaraṇapaddhati is a ritual manual (paddhati) for the worship and remembrance of Caitanya and Govinda (Kṛṣṇa) within the Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava tradition. It is a technical text prescribing ritual procedures and is of interest primarily to specialists in Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava ritual practice.

title: Prajñāpāramitāpiṇḍārtha
author: Diṅnāga
classification_notes: The Prajñāpāramitāpiṇḍārtha by Diṅnāga (ca. 816 tokens) is a concise summary of the Prajñāpāramitā sūtras composed by the renowned Buddhist logician. Essential for understanding the intersection of Buddhist philosophy and sūtra exegesis, it is a specialist text within Buddhist studies.

title: Prajñāpāramitāpiṇḍārtha
author: Diṅnāga
classification_notes: The Prajñāpāramitāpiṇḍārtha by Diṅnāga is a concise 731-word compendium of the Prajñāpāramitā sūtras, summarizing their philosophical essence. Diṅnāga, better known as a logician and epistemologist, here applies his analytical skill to distill the core teachings on emptiness and the perfection of wisdom. This short work is of specialist interest within the broader Prajñāpāramitā commentarial tradition.

title: Kuṭṭanīmata
author: Dāmodaragupta
classification_notes: Dāmodaragupta's Kuṭṭanīmata (The Advice of the Courtesan) is an early medieval Sanskrit kāvya poem, composed around the 8th–9th century in the form of a didactic narrative on the arts of love and courtesanship. It is a notable work in the śṛṅgāra (erotic) genre of kāvya, valued by specialists in Sanskrit literature and social history.

title: Kuṭṭanīmata
author: Dāmodaragupta
classification_notes: Kuṭṭanīmata (The Advice of a Courtesan) is a Sanskrit kavya by Dāmodaragupta, written in the 8th century. It is a witty narrative poem on the life of a courtesan, notable for its social commentary and poetic skill.

title: Kauśikasūtrabhāṣya
author: Dārila
classification_notes: The Kauśikasūtrabhāṣya by Dārila is a commentary on the Kauśika Sūtra, a domestic ritual manual of the Atharvaveda. Its 8,307 words represent a moderately sized exegetical work. Essential for advanced study of Vedic domestic ritual but specialist in the broader Sanskrit field.

title: Kauśikasūtrabhāṣya
author: Dārila
classification_notes: The Kauśikasūtrabhāṣya, authored by Dārila, is an early commentary on the Kauśika Sūtra, the Gṛhyasūtra and ritual manual of the Atharvaveda. It is an essential exegetical tool for understanding the domestic and magical rituals of the Atharvavedic tradition. While indispensable for Atharvaveda specialists, the commentary's highly technical content and its niche place within Vedic studies situate it firmly in the specialist category.

title: Kārikā
author: Gauḍapāda
classification_notes: The Kārikā (often called the Gauḍapādīya Kārikā or Māṇḍūkya Kārikā) by Gauḍapāda is a foundational text of the Advaita Vedānta tradition. It is a verse commentary on the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad, expounding a rigorous non-dualist philosophy. Its length (c. 3,215 words) is concise but highly influential. It is a core text in Indian philosophy curricula, and its ideas have had a profound impact on later Vedānta, placing it in the 'common' popularity tier even within the broader Sanskrit corpus.

title: Kārikā
author: Gauḍapāda
classification_notes: The Māṇḍūkyopaniṣatkārikā is a foundational text of Vedanta philosophy by Gauḍapāda, presenting a commentary in verse form on the Māṇḍūkya Upaniṣad. It is a common text across the Sanskrit philosophical corpus and a major work within the Vedanta tradition.

title: Āgamaśāstra
author: Gauḍapāda
classification_notes: Gauḍapāda’s Āgamaśāstra is a foundational text of the Advaita Vedanta tradition, composed in verse. It synthesizes Upaniṣadic thought with Buddhist and other philosophical influences, and its influence on Śaṅkara is profound.

title: Āgamaśāstra
author: Gauḍapāda
classification_notes: Classification: The 'Āgamaśāstra' of Gauḍapāda is a foundational Vedantic text presenting the doctrine of non-duality (advaita) in verse form. It is a common text read widely by students of Indian philosophy and is major within the Vedanta discovery group, second only to the classical Upaniṣads in prominence.

title: Gorakṣaśataka
author: Gorakṣanātha
classification_notes: Gorakṣanātha's Gorakṣaśataka (One Hundred Verses of Gorakṣa) is a foundational text of the Nātha Siddha tradition, attributed to the legendary yogi Gorakṣanātha. This work (1,492 words) is a concise manual on Haṭha Yoga and Tantric practices. It is a key text for the study of the Nātha tradition and the history of yoga, placing it in the specialist tier globally and within the religion discovery group.

title: Gorakṣaśataka
author: Gorakṣanātha
classification_notes: The Gorakṣaśataka, attributed to the legendary yogi Gorakṣanātha, is a foundational Hatha Yoga text composed in medieval Sanskrit. It presents systematic teachings on yogic practices including postures, breath control, and meditation, and is central to the Nātha yogic tradition. While known within yoga and tantric studies, it holds specialist rather than canonical standing in the broader Sanskrit corpus.

title: Āryāsaptaśatī
author: Govardhana
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Kavya; dcs_time_slot=medieval; dcs_author=Govardhana; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Āryāsaptaśatī by Govardhana (ca. 8061 tokens) is a celebrated medieval Sanskrit kāvya comprising 700 verses in the āryā meter, focused on erotic and courtly themes. It is a widely anthologized work in Sanskrit poetry, commonly read by students and scholars alike.

title: Pradeśasaṃgraha
author: Govind Kaul
classification_notes: Govind Kaul's Pradeśasaṃgraha appears to be a collection of devotional verses or hymns, likely in the Śaiva tradition. The work's content and genre are not fully determined, but it represents the regional Kashmiri Śaiva literary output.

title: Pradeśasaṃgraha
author: Govind Kaul
classification_notes: The Pradeśasaṃgraha by Govind Kaul is a medieval Sanskrit work belonging to the Śaiva Tantra tradition, compiling teachings or practices (pradeśa). Text of about 10,000 words, it is known chiefly within the specialist domain of Kashmir Shaivism and tantric studies. Its precise date and textual history require further research.

title: Tattvacintāmaṇi
author: Gāṅgeśa
classification_notes: The Tattvacintāmaṇi (The Jewel of Thought on Reality) by Gāṅgeśa is the foundational text of the Navya-Nyāya (New Logic) school. This work on epistemology and logic revolutionized Indian philosophy. The Śabdakhaṇḍa section deals with the philosophy of language. It is a demanding but central text for the study of Indian logic and epistemology.

title: Tattvacintāmaṇi
author: Gāṅgeśa
classification_notes: The Tattvacintāmaṇi ('Gem of True Thought') by Gāṅgeśa is the foundational text of the Navya-Nyāya (New Logic) school. This work, specifically the Śabdakhaṇḍa (section on verbal knowledge), revolutionized Indian logic and epistemology with its rigorous analytical methods. It is a cornerstone of medieval Indian philosophy and is essential for specialists in Nyāya and the history of logic.

title: Nyāyapraveśapravṛtti
author: Haribhadrasūri
classification_notes: Haribhadrasūri's Nyāyapraveśapravṛtti is a Jain philosophical work that introduces logic and epistemology, adapting the structure of Dignāga's Nyāyapraveśa. The text is a key source for understanding the reception of Buddhist logic within the Jain tradition. Its primary audience is specialists in Jain philosophy and Indian logic.

title: Nyāyapraveśapravṛtti
author: Haribhadrasūri
classification_notes: Haribhadrasūri's Nyāyapraveśapravṛtti is a Jain primer on logic (nyāya) that presents the principles of reasoning from a Jain perspective. It is an accessible introduction to Jain epistemology and debate, and is valued in both Jain and broader Indian philosophical curricula.

title: Śāstravārttāsamuccaya
author: Haribhadrasūri
classification_notes: Haribhadrasūri's Śāstravārttāsamuccaya is a compendium of philosophical doctrines (darśanas) from a Jain perspective. It systematically surveys and critiques the major schools of Indian thought, including Buddhism, Mīmāṃsā, and Sāṃkhya. It is a valuable text for the history of Indian philosophy and Jain scholasticism, widely used in specialist circles.

title: Śāstravārttāsamuccaya
author: Haribhadrasūri
classification_notes: The Śāstravārttāsamuccaya by Haribhadrasūri is a Jain philosophical compendium that surveys and critiques various Indian philosophical schools (darśanas) from a Jain perspective. This 10,595-word work is a significant doxographical text in the Jain tradition, demonstrating Haribhadrasūri's synthetic and dialectical method. It is of specialist interest for scholars of Indian philosophy and Jain intellectual history.

title: Satyasiddhiśāstram
author: Harivarman
classification_notes: Satyasiddhiśāstram is a Buddhist philosophical work by Harivarman, setting forth a system of truth. It is influential especially in East Asian Buddhist scholasticism, though not as universally read as Nāgārjuna's works.

title: Satyasiddhiśāstram
author: Harivarman
classification_notes: Classification: The 'Satyasiddhiśāstram' of Harivarman is an important Buddhist scholastic treatise that synthesizes Ābhidharmika and early Mahāyāna ideas. It is specialist overall but common within Buddhist philosophical circles, occupying a distinctive place in the Sautrāntika and Mādhyamaka traditions.

title: Harivaṃśa
author: Harivaṃśa
classification_notes: The Harivaṃśa (ca. 158,883 tokens) is a seminal epic supplement to the Mahābhārata, recounting the genealogy and exploits of Kṛṣṇa. It is a canonical work of Sanskrit epic literature and Hindu mythology, widely read across the tradition.

title: Harivaṃśa
author: Harivaṃśa
classification_notes: Traditionally considered an appendix (khila) to the Mahābhārata, the Harivaṃśa is a substantial work of nearly 147,000 words that narrates the life of Kṛṣṇa and the lineage of the Vṛṣṇi clan. It is a key text for the mythology of Kṛṣṇa and is widely read within the Hindu tradition, though its reach is slightly less than that of the main epic. It is a major work in the Sanskrit epic corpus.

title: Nāgānanda
author: Harṣadeva
classification_notes: The Nāgānanda (Joy of the Serpents) is a celebrated nāṭaka by King Harṣadeva, blending Buddhist themes with classical Sanskrit dramaturgy. The play narrates the self-sacrifice of the Bodhisattva Jīmūtavāhana. It is widely appreciated for its lyrical beauty and ethical depth, holding a secure place in the Sanskrit dramatic canon.

title: Nāgānanda
author: Harṣadeva
classification_notes: Harṣadeva's Nāgānanda (Joy of the Serpents) is a celebrated Sanskrit nāṭaka (drama) in six acts, traditionally attributed to the emperor Harṣavardhana (7th century). The play combines a Buddhist narrative about the self-sacrifice of the serpent king Śaṅkhacūḍa with elements of classical Sanskrit dramaturgy. It is a well-known work in the study of Indian theatre and literature.

title: Abhidhānacintāmaṇi
author: Hemacandra
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Lexicography; dcs_subject=Kosha; dcs_time_slot=medieval; dcs_author=Hemacandra; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Hemacandra's Abhidhānacintāmaṇi is a Sanskrit thesaurus composed by the renowned Jain polymath. It arranges synonyms by topic and is a key text in the Sanskrit lexicographical tradition (kośa). Within the field of Sanskrit lexicography it is a common reference, though its overall readership in the broader Sanskrit corpus is specialist.

title: Prāṇābharaṇa
author: Jagannātha
classification_notes: The Prāṇābharaṇa by Jagannātha is a Śaiva Tantric text on vital forces (prāṇa). It is a specialist work within the tantric literary tradition.

title: Prāṇābharaṇa
author: Jagannātha
classification_notes: The Prāṇābharaṇa by Jagannātha is a brief alchemical-medical work (Rasashastra) of 921 words, likely composed in the medieval period. It deals with the preparation and application of mercurial and mineral-based rasāyana remedies. The text is obscure and accessible primarily to specialists in the history of Indian alchemy and iatrochemistry. Its short length and technical focus limit its broader relevance.

title: Prāṇābharaṇaṭippaṇī
author: Jagannātha
classification_notes: Prāṇābharaṇaṭippaṇī is a Sanskrit commentary by Jagannātha on the Prāṇābharaṇa, a text likely dealing with yoga or tantra. It is a specialized secondary work.

title: Prāṇābharaṇaṭippaṇī
author: Jagannātha
classification_notes: Jagannātha’s Prāṇābharaṇaṭippaṇī is a brief commentary on a Haṭha Yoga text, composed in the early modern period. It offers insight into the reception of yoga practice in scholarly circles.

title: Sudhālaharī
author: Jagannātha
classification_notes: The Sudhālaharī (Wave of Nectar) is a lyrical poem by Jagannātha Paṇḍitarāja, a celebrated poet and scholar of the Mughal court. It is a short, elegant kāvya piece, often classified as a stotra or praise poem. While Jagannātha is a major figure in late Sanskrit literature, this particular work is a minor gem, read mainly by connoisseurs of ornate poetry and specialists in the early modern Sanskrit renaissance.

title: Sudhālaharī
author: Jagannātha
classification_notes: Jagannātha's Sudhālaharī is a Sanskrit stotra (devotional hymn) composed by the celebrated poet Jagannātha Paṇḍitarāja, a prominent figure at the Mughal court. The poem, a 'wave of nectar', is a late example of ornate kāvya and is of primary interest to specialists in Sanskrit poetry and devotional literature from the early modern period.

title: Mīmāṃsāsūtra
author: Jaimini
classification_notes: The Mīmāṃsāsūtra, attributed to the philosopher Jaimini, is the foundational text of the Mīmāṃsā school of Vedic exegesis and ritual hermeneutics. With 17,145 words, it systematically sets forth the principles for interpreting the Vedas, establishing the rules of Vedic ritual and the nature of dharma. It is one of the six classical systems of Indian philosophy and a core text for understanding Brahmanical orthopraxy.

title: Mīmāṃsāsūtra
author: Jaimini
classification_notes: The Mīmāṃsāsūtra, attributed to Jaimini, is the foundational text of the Mīmāṃsā school of Vedic exegesis and ritual hermeneutics. Composed in sutra style, this work systematically investigates the nature of dharma as derived from Vedic injunction. It is a core philosophical text within the classical Indian tradition, widely studied for its rigorous analysis of language, meaning, and ritual obligation. The text's word count of 19,198 reflects a compact yet dense exposition, typical of the sutra genre.

title: Mugdhopadeśa
author: Jalhaṇa
classification_notes: Mugdhopadeśa is a satirical poetic work by the Jain author Jalhaṇa, offering moral instruction through wit and humor. It is an example of didactic kavya with a distinctive style.

title: Mugdhopadeśa
author: Jalhaṇa
classification_notes: Mugdhopadeśa is a short didactic and humorous poem by Jalhaṇa offering worldly advice on matters of love and cunning. It is a specialist work within Sanskrit kavya and little known outside the field.

title: Candrāloka
author: Jayadeva
classification_notes: Jayadeva's Candrāloka is a celebrated medieval Sanskrit treatise on poetics (alaṃkāraśāstra), attributed to the same Jayadeva who composed the Gītagovinda. The work systematically expounds figures of speech, poetic qualities, and rhetorical theory through concise definitions and illustrative verses. It is widely used in traditional Sanskrit pedagogy for alamkāra and is a common reference in the field of Sanskrit poetics. Nonetheless, its specialist status in the global corpus arises from the technical and school-specific nature of the discipline.

title: Candrāloka
author: Jayadeva
classification_notes: Jayadeva's Candrāloka is a Classical Sanskrit treatise on poetics and rhetoric, specifically the figures of speech (alamkara). As a standard textbook on Sanskrit poetics, it is well-known within the field of Sanskrit literary criticism (common within its group), but its specialized nature limits its global popularity to a specialist audience.

title: Gītagovinda
author: Jayadeva
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Kavya; dcs_time_slot=medieval; dcs_author=Jayadeva; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Gītagovinda (Song of the Cowherd) is a world-renowned lyrical poem by the late 12th-century poet Jayadeva. Composed of 3,979 words of exquisite Sanskrit verse, it narrates the love of Rādhā and Kṛṣṇa, fusing refined kāvya aesthetics with fervent bhakti devotion. It is a masterpiece of both Sanskrit literature and Vaishnava devotionalism, widely performed, translated, and studied across the world.

title: Sphuṭārthā Śrīghanācārasaṃgrahaṭīkā
author: Jayarakṣita
classification_notes: Jayarakṣita's Sphuṭārthā Śrīghanācārasaṃgrahaṭīkā is a commentary on the Śrīghanācārasaṃgraha, a Buddhist tantric text. As a specialized commentary within a specific tantric lineage, its readership is restricted to scholars of Buddhist tantra, placing it in the specialist tier.

title: Sphuṭārthā Śrīghanācārasaṃgrahaṭīkā
author: Jayarakṣita
classification_notes: Jayarakṣita's Sphuṭārthā is a commentary (ṭīkā) on the Śrīghanācārasaṃgraha, a medieval digest of conduct and ritual from the Śaiva tradition. As a specialist scholastic work intended for initiated readers, its readership is limited to researchers of Śaiva dharmaśāstra and the siddhānta traditions. It is relatively obscure in the broader Sanskrit corpus.

title: Tattvopaplavasiṃha
author: Jayarāśi
classification_notes: Jayarāśi's Tattvopaplavasiṃha is a radical sceptical text in the Cārvāka tradition, arguing for the complete unreliability of knowledge sources. It is a unique and important work for the study of Indian scepticism, but its philosophical iconoclasm and narrow manuscript tradition make it a specialist work read mainly by historians of Indian philosophy.

title: Tattvopaplavasiṃha
author: Jayarāśi
classification_notes: Jayarāśi's Tattvopaplavasiṃha is a singularly important work of Sanskrit philosophy, representing a radical skeptical (Cārvāka/Lokāyata) critique of epistemology and metaphysics. Attributed with confidence to the philosopher Jayarāśi, this text is a cornerstone for the study of Indian skepticism, though its specialized subject matter and the relative obscurity of the Cārvāka tradition within the broader Sanskrit corpus limit its global popularity to the specialist tier. Within the philosophy discovery group, it is a major work for specialists in Indian philosophy, but not widely read outside that field.

title: Hetutattvopadeśa
author: Jitāri
classification_notes: The Hetutattvopadeśa by Jitāri is a philosophical work on the nature of causality and inference, written from a Buddhist epistemological perspective. Jitāri is known as a Yogācāra scholar (10th–11th century). This text is a specialist source for the study of Buddhist logic and pramāṇa theory.

title: Hetutattvopadeśa
author: Jitāri
classification_notes: Jitāri's Hetutattvopadeśa is a concise Buddhist philosophical treatise on the nature of causality and reasoning, composed in the early medieval period. As a work of Buddhist logic and epistemology, it engages with Nyāya categories from a Buddhist perspective. The text is of specialist interest within Buddhist philosophy and Indian logic, and its moderate length (2193 words) suggests a focused, possibly pedagogical, exposition.

title: Rājataraṅginī
author: Jonarāja, Pseudo-Jonarāja
classification_notes: The Rājataraṅginī (River of Kings) is the seminal historical chronicle of Kashmir by Jonarāja and later Pseudo-Jonarāja. As one of the few Sanskrit historical works, it is widely consulted by historians of medieval India. Its status as a unique historical source gives it common popularity within the Sanskrit corpus.

title: Pañcasāyaka
author: Jyotirīśvarakaviśekhara
classification_notes: Pañcasāyaka is a Sanskrit poetic treatise on erotics (kamashastra) attributed to Jyotirīśvarakaviśekhara. Composed in the early medieval period, it is a technical work in verse, primarily of interest to specialists in Sanskrit erotics and kavya.

title: Pañcasāyaka
author: Jyotirīśvarakaviśekhara
classification_notes: The Pañcasāyaka ('Five Arrows') by Jyotirīśvarakaviśekhara is a medieval Sanskrit work on erotics (kāmaśāstra), attributed with confidence. With ca. 5146 tokens, it is a specialist text within the technical tradition of kāmaśāstra, consulted primarily by scholars of Sanskrit erotics and medieval Indian culture.

title: Rādhākṛṣṇārcanadīpikā
author: Jīvagosvāmin
classification_notes: The Rādhākṛṣṇārcanadīpikā by Jīvagosvāmin is a Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava manual on the ritual worship of Rādhā and Kṛṣṇa, composed within the circle of the Six Gosvāmins of Vrindavan. It is an obscure text read primarily in Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava liturgical contexts.

title: Rādhākṛṣṇārcanadīpikā
author: Jīvagosvāmin
classification_notes: The Rādhākṛṣṇārcanadīpikā by Jīvagosvāmin is a 5,251-word Bengali Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava manual on the ritual worship of Rādhā and Kṛṣṇa, composed in Sanskrit in the 16th–17th century CE. It blends stotra, ritual prescription, and bhakti theology. The text is specialist in the Sanskrit corpus and specialist within stotra literature.

title: Saṃkalpakalpadruma
author: Jīvagosvāmin
classification_notes: Jīvagosvāmin's Saṃkalpakalpadruma is a theological treatise in the Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava tradition, discussing the nature of divine will and devotion. It is a significant work for the study of Caitanya Vaiṣṇava philosophy.

title: Saṃkalpakalpadruma
author: Jīvagosvāmin
classification_notes: Jīvagosvāmin's Saṃkalpakalpadruma (The Wish-fulfilling Tree of Resolve) is a devotional work from the Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava tradition, composed in the 16th century. It is a substantial text (about 12,660 words) of hymns and theological exposition centered on Kṛṣṇa bhakti. It is an important source for the study of Caitanya Vaiṣṇavism and medieval Bengali Sanskrit literature.

title: Ardhanarīśvarastotra
author: Kalhaṇa
classification_notes: The Ardhanarīśvarastotra by Kalhaṇa is a brief Sanskrit hymn praising the androgynous form of Śiva (Ardhanārīśvara). Its short length and niche devotional content place it among obscure works in the global Sanskrit corpus, with interest largely confined to specialists in Śaiva stotra literature.

title: Ardhanarīśvarastotra
author: Kalhaṇa
classification_notes: Kalhaṇa's Ardhanarīśvarastotra is a short devotional hymn to Ardhanarīśvara, the androgynous form of Śiva and Pārvatī. It is a minor but elegant example of Sanskrit stotra literature.

title: Madhyamakāloka
author: Kamalaśīla
classification_notes: Kamalaśīla's Madhyamakāloka is a philosophical work from the Indian Buddhist tradition, presenting a synthesis of Madhyamaka and Yogācāra thought. It is an important text within the later Indian Buddhist scholastic tradition, composed by a key figure at Nālandā. Its readership is mainly among specialists in Buddhist philosophy.

title: Madhyamakāloka
author: Kamalaśīla
classification_notes: The Madhyamakāloka by Kamalaśīla (c. 8th–9th century CE) is a 28,101-word Sanskrit text on Madhyamaka Buddhist philosophy, presenting a synthesis of Śāntarakṣita's Yogācāra-Madhyamaka. The work is specialist in the Sanskrit corpus and specialist within Buddhist scripture and philosophy.

title: Navaślokī
author: Kambalapāda
classification_notes: The Navaślokī by Kambalapāda is a terse Buddhist Tantric text of 195 words, presenting nine ślokas on the nature of mind and emptiness, likely from the 8th–10th century CE. As an independent short work, it is highly specialist in the Sanskrit corpus and specialist within Buddhist tantra.

title: Navaślokī
author: Kambalapāda
classification_notes: The Navaślokī by Kambalapāda is a 222-word Buddhist Sanskrit hymn (stotra) focusing on the nine verses of praise to the Buddha and bodhisattvas. Attributed to the master Kambalapāda, a key figure in the Yogācāra and Tantric Buddhist traditions, this short text is a specialist devotional piece within the broader Sanskrit Buddhist literary tradition.

title: Sāṃkhyasūtra
author: Kapila
classification_notes: The Sāṃkhyasūtra ascribed to Kapila is the foundational aphoristic text of the classical Sāṃkhya school, though its redaction likely occurred in the later medieval period. Its 3,216-word text is essential for the study of Sāṃkhya but is specialized in its audience.

title: Sāṃkhyasūtra
author: Kapila
classification_notes: The Sāṃkhyasūtra (4,192 words) attributed to Kapila is a variant edition of the foundational Sāṃkhya aphorisms. Its 4,192-word form is a standard text in the history of Sāṃkhya, though its audience remains specialist.

title: Arthaśāstra
author: Kauṭilya
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Technical Literature; dcs_subject=Arthashastra; dcs_time_slot=epic; dcs_author=Kauṭilya; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Arthaśāstra of Kauṭilya is a seminal treatise on statecraft, economics, and political strategy. It is one of the most important works of ancient Indian political thought and a major text in the Arthaśāstra tradition. Its impact extends beyond philosophy into law, history, and economics, making it a widely studied work across many disciplines.

title: Sāṃkhyatattvapradīpa
author: Kavirājayati
classification_notes: A commentary on the Sāṃkhya system by Kavirājayati, the Sāṃkhyatattvapradīpa ('Light on the Truths of Sāṃkhya') is an exposition of the principles of Sāṃkhya philosophy. It is a relatively minor work in the commentarial tradition, of interest primarily to scholars specializing in the history of Indian philosophy.

title: Sāṃkhyatattvapradīpa
author: Kavirājayati
classification_notes: Kavirājayati's Sāṃkhyatattvapradīpa is a medieval commentary that elucidates the classical Sāṃkhya system. It serves as an important exegetical work for understanding the evolution of Sāṃkhya thought in the early medieval period. While its readership is largely confined to specialists in Indian philosophy, its concise exposition (ca. 2800 words) makes it a valuable pedagogical text within the tradition.

title: Vṛttaratnākara
author: Kedārabhaṭṭa
classification_notes: Kedārabhaṭṭa's Vṛttaratnākara is a standard classical Sanskrit handbook on metre (chandas). It is widely used in traditional Sanskrit education and by scholars of poetics, offering a systematic exposition of Sanskrit verse forms. Its popularity extends well beyond a narrow specialty, making it a commonly encountered work within the broader Sanskrit curriculum.

title: Vṛttaratnākara
author: Kedārabhaṭṭa
classification_notes: Kedārabhaṭṭa's Vṛttaratnākara is a seminal treatise on Sanskrit prosody. It systematically classifies meters and is a standard reference for poets and scholars of Sanskrit metrics.

title: Kauśikasūtrapaddhati
author: Keśava
classification_notes: The Kauśikasūtrapaddhati by Keśava is a 66,795-word commentary (paddhati) on the Kauśika Sūtra, the ritual manual of the Atharvaveda. This medieval exegetical text explains and expands upon the Vedic domestic and magical rites of the Atharvavedic tradition. It is a highly detailed work of interest to specialists in Vedic ritual, particularly the Atharvaveda, and the history of the Kalpa-sūtra genre.

title: Kauśikasūtrapaddhati
author: Keśava
classification_notes: The Kauśikasūtrapaddhati by Keśava is a medieval commentary on the Kauśikasūtra, a ritual text of the Atharvaveda dealing with domestic and magical rites. The work is specialist, serving scholars of Vedic ritual and exegesis.

title: Sāṃkhyatattvapradīpikā
author: Keśava
classification_notes: Keśava's Sāṃkhyatattvapradīpikā is a commentary on the Sāṃkhya tradition, elucidating its principles. As a relatively late exposition, its readership is mainly among specialists in Sāṃkhya and Indian philosophy. Its limited circulation and niche subject matter place it firmly in the specialist tier within the global Sanskrit corpus.

title: Sāṃkhyatattvapradīpikā
author: Keśava
classification_notes: Keśava’s Sāṃkhyatattvapradīpikā is a concise technical manual of Sāṃkhya doctrine (854 words), offering a lucid summary of Sāṃkhyan categories. Its brevity and focus limit it to a specialist readership in the history of Sāṃkhya philosophy.

title: Mukundamālā
author: Kulaśekhara
classification_notes: The Mukundamālā by Kulaśekhara is a popular Sanskrit stotra in praise of Kṛṣṇa, attributed to the Āḻvār saint. It holds a common position within the stotra corpus and is a specialist devotional work in the broader Sanskrit corpus.

title: Mukundamālā
author: Kulaśekhara
classification_notes: Kulaśekhara’s Mukundamālā is a celebrated Vaiṣṇava stotra composed in simple yet eloquent Sanskrit. It expresses deep devotion to Kṛṣṇa and is widely recited in South Indian temples.

title: Kumārasaṃbhava
author: Kālidāsa
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Kavya; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Kālidāsa; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Kumārasaṃbhava by Kālidāsa is one of the greatest masterpieces of Classical Sanskrit kāvya. This 7234-word epic poem narrates the birth and marriage of the war-god Skanda (Kumāra) to the goddess Pārvatī. Renowned for its lyrical beauty, sophisticated imagery, and profound emotional range, it is a cornerstone of the Sanskrit literary canon and a major text in Indian aesthetic education.

title: Meghadūta
author: Kālidāsa
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Kavya; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Kālidāsa; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Kālidāsa's Meghadūta is one of the most beloved and widely studied Sanskrit poems, a masterpiece of the kāvya genre. Composed as a lyrical message poem (dūtakāvya) of ca. 2200 words, it epitomizes classical Sanskrit literary aesthetics. Its canonical status ensures its presence on curricula worldwide, and it enjoys a readership well beyond specialist confines.

title: Raghuvaṃśa
author: Kālidāsa
classification_notes: Kālidāsa's Raghuvaṃśa (The Dynasty of Raghu) is the supreme masterpiece of Sanskrit mahākāvya (courtly epic), tracing the lineage of the solar dynasty from Dilīpa to the later descendants of Rāma. Composed in the 4th–5th century CE, it is a cornerstone of classical Indian literature, admired for its poetic beauty, historical scope, and structural artistry. It is universally canonical in Indological studies.

title: Raghuvaṃśa
author: Kālidāsa
classification_notes: Kālidāsa's Raghuvaṃśa is a classical Sanskrit mahākāvya (epic poem) narrating the lineage of the Raghu dynasty. It is one of the most celebrated works of Sanskrit literature, widely studied for its poetic brilliance and cultural significance.

title: Bhāvanāsārasaṃgraha
author: Kṛṣṇadāsa
classification_notes: The Bhāvanāsārasaṃgraha by Kṛṣṇadāsa is a compendium of meditative practices and devotional contemplation, likely from a Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava context. It collects verses and instructions on bhāvanā, serving as a manual for spiritual practice.

title: Bhāvanāsārasaṃgraha
author: Kṛṣṇadāsa
classification_notes: The Bhāvanāsārasaṃgraha by Kṛṣṇadāsa is a medieval Sanskrit devotional compendium, likely associated with the Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava tradition. Its 11,550 tokens collect and synthesize contemplative themes (bhāvanā) centered on Kṛṣṇa devotion. The work is specialist within the broader Sanskrit corpus but holds a common place among stotra and bhakti literature, reflecting sustained use in devotional communities.

title: Bhairavānukaraṇastotra
author: Kṣemarāja
classification_notes: The Bhairavānukaraṇastotra by Kṣemarāja is a devotional hymn (stotra) in praise of Bhairava, a fierce form of Śiva. Kṣemarāja was a prominent philosopher and theologian of the Pratyabhijñā school of Kashmir Śaivism. This short but theologically rich text reflects the tantric and devotional currents of early medieval Śaivism and is of interest to specialists in Śaiva literature and philosophy.

title: Bhairavānukaraṇastotra
author: Kṣemarāja
classification_notes: The Bhairavānukaraṇastotra is a devotional hymn by Kṣemarāja, a key figure in the Pratyabhijñā school of Kashmir Śaivism. The stotra praises Bhairava, a fierce form of Śiva, and is embedded in the tantric tradition of the Śaiva āgamas. It is a specialist text for those studying the devotional and liturgical aspects of Kashmir Śaivism.

title: Bodhavilāsa
author: Kṣemarāja
classification_notes: The Bodhavilāsa by Kṣemarāja is a 615-word philosophical hymn or short treatise celebrating the bliss of spiritual awakening (bodha) from the perspective of Kashmiri Śaivism. Kṣemarāja, a disciple of Abhinavagupta, was a major exponent of the Pratyabhijñā school. This text is a specialist gem for scholars of Śaiva philosophy and the literature of non-dual Kashmir Śaivism.

title: Bodhavilāsa
author: Kṣemarāja
classification_notes: A short Sanskrit hymn (stotra) of 573 words composed by Kṣemarāja, the famous Kashmiri Śaiva philosopher and disciple of Abhinavagupta. The Bodhavilāsa ('Delight of Awakening') is a devotional and philosophical hymn that praises Śiva and expounds the principles of Trika Śaivism. It is a specialist text in the field of Tantric and Śaiva studies.

title: Avadānakalpalatā
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: A collection of Buddhist avadāna legends compiled by the Kashmiri poet Kṣemendra (c. 10th–11th c. CE). The Avadānakalpalatā presents over a hundred stories of the Buddha’s previous lives and virtuous deeds, rendered in elegant kāvya style. While the work is a significant contribution to Buddhist narrative literature in Sanskrit, its readership is largely confined to specialists in Buddhist narrative and the Sanskrit katha tradition.

title: Avadānakalpalatā
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: Kṣemendra's Avadānakalpalatā is a vast collection of Buddhist avadānas (heroic tales of karmic retribution), composed in Sanskrit verse. It systematically retells narratives from the Avadānaśataka and other sources, embedding them in a framework of Buddhist ethics. The work is a major contribution to Sanskrit Buddhist literature and has been widely studied in both Indian and Tibetan traditions.

title: Bhāratamañjarī
author: Kṣemendra

This kāvya-style condensation reworks the epic narrative into a refined literary form, showcasing Kṣemendra's characteristic concision and stylistic elegance. It is a major work within the Sanskrit epic tradition and offers a distinct medieval perspective on the epic's themes.

title: Caturvargasaṃgraha
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: The Caturvargasaṃgraha (Compendium of the Four Aims) is a significant didactic poem by the prolific Kashmiri author Kṣemendra. Comprising about 2,800 words, it systematically presents the four puruṣārthas (aims of human life): dharma (righteousness), artha (wealth), kāma (pleasure), and mokṣa (liberation). Written in elegant kāvya style, it serves as both a literary work and a moral compendium, reflecting Kṣemendra's interest in ethical education.

title: Caturvargasaṃgraha
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: Kṣemendra's Caturvargasaṃgraha is a didactic work in verse that teaches the four aims of life (dharma, artha, kāma, mokṣa). The Kashmiri poet Kṣemendra is known for his abridged versions and ethical poetry. The work is of specialist interest to scholars of Sanskrit didactic literature and Kṣemendra's oeuvre.

title: Darpadalana
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: Kṣemendra's Darpadalana is a satirical kāvya that humorously criticizes human vices. It is part of the author's moralistic oeuvre and is significant for its literary and ethical commentary.

title: Darpadalana
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: Kṣemendra's Darpadalana ('Crushing of Pride') is a satirical poem in the kāvya tradition, exemplifying the author's moralistic and humorous style. It critiques human vanity through vivid character sketches and is valued for its literary artistry and social commentary.

title: Garbhāvakrāntyavadāna
author: Kṣemendra

This short piece is of particular interest to scholars of Buddhist narrative literature and Kṣemendra's oeuvre.

title: Garbhāvakrāntyavadāna
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: Kṣemendra's Garbhāvakrāntyavadāna is a Buddhist narrative (avadāna) recounting stories related to auspicious births. It forms part of Kṣemendra's series of didactic Buddhist tales.

title: Kalāvilāsa
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: Kṣemendra's Kalāvilāsa is a witty satirical poem in the kāvya tradition, offering a critique of contemporary society and human vices through the persona of a clever courtesan. The work blends literary elegance with moral instruction and is representative of Kṣemendra's didactic and ironic style. It has been studied as an example of classical Sanskrit social satire.

title: Kalāvilāsa
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: Kalāvilāsa is a satirical narrative poem by Kṣemendra that humorously critiques frauds and charlatans of various professions. It is a specialist work in the broader Sanskrit corpus and within the subgenre of satirical kavya.

title: Pratītyasamutpādāvadāna
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: Pratītyasamutpādāvadāna is a short Buddhist narrative by the prolific Kashmirian poet Kṣemendra, illustrating the doctrine of dependent origination through a story. It exemplifies the blending of Buddhist didacticism with kavya literary style.

title: Pratītyasamutpādāvadāna
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: A concise Buddhist avadāna on dependent origination (pratītyasamutpāda) attributed to Kṣemendra. The work is a short narrative of only 167 words, focusing on a core Buddhist doctrine. Its extreme brevity and niche topic make it an obscure text even within the specialist study of Buddhist narrative literature.

title: Sevyasevakopadeśaḥ
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: Kṣemendra's Sevyasevakopadeśaḥ (Advice to Master and Servant) is a didactic kāvya work by the prolific 11th-century Kashmiri poet Kṣemendra. This short poem (1,237 words) offers moral and practical instruction on social relationships. While Kṣemendra is a well-known author, this particular work is less prominent than his major narrative cycles, placing it in the specialist tier. Within the ethics discovery group, it is a minor but characteristic example of Sanskrit didactic poetry.

title: Sevyasevakopadeśaḥ
author: Kṣemendra
