title: Sudhanakinnaryavadāna
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: Kṣemendra’s Sudhanakinnaryavadāna is a lyrical Buddhist avadāna recounting the story of the prince Sudhana and the kinnarī. It exemplifies the later kāvya style infused with Buddhist themes.

title: Sudhanakinnaryavadāna
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: Kṣemendra's Sudhanakinnaryavadāna is a Buddhist narrative poem recounting the story of Sudhana and the Kinnari. It belongs to the kāvya tradition with a Buddhist theme, composed by the polymath Kṣemendra in 11th-century Kashmir. Within Buddhist narrative literature, it is moderately known, but it remains a specialist work even among Kṣemendra's oeuvre.

title: Sumāgadhāvadāna
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: The Sumāgadhāvadāna is a Buddhist avadāna (heroic tale) attributed to the Kashmiri polymath Kṣemendra, forming part of his larger Bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatā. This narrative recounts the story of Sumāgadhā, a laywoman who demonstrates faith and virtue. Within Kṣemendra's corpus and among Buddhist narrative literature, it holds a specialist appeal, being read primarily by scholars of Sanskrit Buddhist storytelling and kāvya. Its length (c. 1,775 words) is typical of an individual avadāna tale.

title: Sumāgadhāvadāna
author: Kṣemendra
classification_notes: Kṣemendra's Sumāgadhāvadāna is a Buddhist narrative (avadāna) from his larger collection, the Bodhisattvāvadānakalpalatā. This work (1,646 words) recounts a story of a previous life of the Buddha. As a part of Kṣemendra's vast narrative cycle, it is a specialist text, of interest primarily to scholars of Buddhist narrative literature and the works of Kṣemendra. Its global popularity is specialist, and within the religion discovery group it is a minor text.

title: Larger Prajñāpāramitā
author: Larger
classification_notes: The Larger Prajñāpāramitā is a major Mahayana sutra representing the 'Perfection of Wisdom' genre. As a foundational text for Mahayana Buddhist philosophy, it is widely studied and revered. It has a common level of global popularity, being a core text for scholars and practitioners of Buddhism.

title: Śivastuti
author: Laṅkeśvara
classification_notes: The Śivastuti by Laṅkeśvara is a short medieval Sanskrit hymn in praise of Śiva. As a minor stotra from a less prominent author, this work is of interest mainly to specialists in Śaiva devotional literature and Sanskrit hymnody. Its very short length (269 words) and narrow transmission contribute to its obscure global popularity.

title: Śivastuti
author: Laṅkeśvara
classification_notes: Laṅkeśvara's Śivastuti is a brief hymn of praise to Śiva (ca. 160 words), typical of the stotra genre. The author is otherwise little-known, and the text survives in a limited manuscript tradition. It is of marginal interest even within stotra studies, serving as a minor specimen of medieval Śaiva devotional poetry.

title: Vaidyajīvana
author: Lolimbarāja
classification_notes: Lolimbarāja's Vaidyajīvana is an early modern Sanskrit medical treatise in the Ayurvedic tradition. The work systematically covers therapeutic formulations and diagnostics, reflecting the practical and applied nature of post-classical Ayurveda. Its moderate length (ca. 4500 words) and structured presentation make it a useful reference within the specialist medical corpus, though it is less known outside ayurvedic scholarship.

title: Vaidyajīvana
author: Lolimbarāja
classification_notes: Lolimbarāja's Vaidyajīvana is a concise medieval treatise on Āyurvedic medicine, covering topics such as pharmacology, diagnosis, and treatment. It is a practical manual intended for physicians and has been used in traditional Indian medical education. While not as renowned as the classical saṃhitās, it represents the later tradition of Āyurvedic compendia.

title: Lāṭyāyana Śrautasūtra
author: Lāṭyāyana
classification_notes: The Lāṭyāyana Śrautasūtra is a ritual sūtra of the Sāmavedic tradition, detailing public sacrificial procedures. It is a specialist text indispensable for the study of Vedic ritual but obscure beyond this field.

title: Siddhāntabindu
author: Madhusūdanasarasvatī
classification_notes: Madhusūdanasarasvatī's Siddhāntabindu is a concise Vedantic treatise that systematically presents the tenets of Advaita Vedānta, often serving as a primer or summary of Śaṅkara's non-dualist philosophy. It engages with key concepts such as māyā, brahman, and ātman, and refutes opposing schools. The work is valued for its clarity and pedagogical structure, making it a standard text for students of Advaita thought.

title: Siddhāntabindu
author: Madhusūdanasarasvatī
classification_notes: Madhusūdanasarasvatī’s Siddhāntabindu is a compact Vedantic compendium that systematically presents the central tenets of Advaita Vedānta, often used as a pedagogical introduction. Although influential within Advaita scholarship, its 6,421-word compass places it among common works in the broader Sanskrit philosophical corpus.

title: Mahāsubhāṣitasaṃgraha
author: Mahāsubhāṣitasaṃgraha
classification_notes: The Mahāsubhāṣitasaṃgraha (Great Collection of Well-Spoken Verses) is a large anthology of Sanskrit didactic and gnomic verse (subhāṣita), containing over 200,000 words. It is a composite compilation, drawing on centuries of ethical, political, and moral poetry. Such anthologies are important sources for the study of popular wisdom, literary trends, and the transmission of verses in pre-modern India.

title: Madhyāntavibhāgakārikā
author: Maitreya
classification_notes: The Madhyāntavibhāgakārikā (Verses on the Distinction of the Middle and Extremes), attributed to Maitreya, is a foundational text of the Yogācāra school. This 1,599-word work delineates the distinction between the middle path (madhyama) and the erroneous extremes. It is a key treatise in Buddhist philosophy, widely studied in the Mahāyāna tradition for its analysis of mind, emptiness, and phenomenal reality.

title: Madhyāntavibhāgakārikā
author: Maitreya
classification_notes: The Madhyāntavibhāgakārikā (Verses on Distinguishing the Middle and the Extremes) is a foundational Yogācāra Buddhist philosophical text attributed to the Bodhisattva Maitreya (or more specifically to the figure of Maitreyanātha). In about 1,300 words of precise kārikās, it delineates the key Yogācāra doctrines of emptiness, consciousness-only, and the path to enlightenment. It is a widely cited text in Buddhist studies.

title: Maitreyavyākaraṇa
author: Maitreyavyākaraṇa
classification_notes: The Maitreyavyākaraṇa (Prophecy of Maitreya) is a Buddhist sūtra or vyākaraṇa text that describes the future advent of the Buddha Maitreya. This short version (about 1,105 words) is a representative of the Maitreya literature in Sanskrit, found in various recensions. It is of interest to specialists in Mahāyāna sūtra literature and eschatological Buddhist texts.

title: Maitreyavyākaraṇa
author: Maitreyavyākaraṇa
classification_notes: The Maitreyavyākaraṇa (1,059 words) is a short Buddhist Sanskrit sūtra predicting the future awakening of the bodhisattva Maitreya. Its specialized content draws the interest of scholars of Buddhist eschatology and Sanskrit Buddhist literature.

title: Maitreyavyākaraṇa
author: Maitreyavyākaraṇa
classification_notes: The Maitreyavyākaraṇa is a Buddhist Sanskrit text belonging to the genre of vyākaraṇa (prophecy or prediction), focusing on the future Buddha Maitreya. It circulates primarily within Buddhist scholarly communities and is relatively obscure outside that field. Its moderate length and specialized content place it among specialist works in the global Sanskrit corpus.

title: Brahmasiddhi
author: Mandanamiśra
classification_notes: The Brahmasiddhi of Maṇḍanamiśra is a key text in the Advaita Vedānta tradition, presenting a philosophically rigorous defense of non-dualism that engages with Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā. It is a substantial work (c. 40,800 words) and is highly regarded within the history of Indian philosophy. However, its technical arguments and specialized audience place it in the specialist tier within the broader Sanskrit corpus, studied primarily by scholars of Vedānta and Indian philosophy.

title: Brahmasiddhi
author: Mandanamiśra
classification_notes: Maṇḍanamiśra's Brahmasiddhi is a major work of Advaita Vedanta philosophy, presenting a systematic defense of non-dualism. It is a key text in the history of Indian philosophy, commonly studied alongside Śaṅkara's works. Its influence on the Vedanta tradition secures it a common popularity tier in the broader Sanskrit corpus.

title: Gautamasmṛtibhāṣya
author: Maskari
classification_notes: The Gautamasmṛtibhāṣya, attributed to Maskari, is a commentary on the Gautama Dharmasūtra, one of the foundational texts of the Dharmasūtra tradition. As a technical legal and ritual commentary, it is of primary interest to specialists in Dharmashastra and ancient Indian law. Its length (c. 12,700 words) is substantial for a commentary on a sūtra text, but its readership is confined largely to academic researchers.

title: Gautamasmṛtibhāṣya
author: Maskari
classification_notes: Maskari’s Gautamasmṛtibhāṣya is a commentary on the Gautamadharmasūtra, a foundational text of Dharmashastra. It provides legal interpretations and is of interest to scholars of ancient Indian law.

title: Vibhramaviveka
author: Maṇḍanamiśra
classification_notes: The Vibhramaviveka by Maṇḍanamiśra is a seminal work in the Advaita Vedanta tradition, analyzing the nature of error (vibhrama) and illusion in perception and epistemology. It is a specialist but important text for the study of Indian philosophy and the history of Advaita thought.

title: Vibhramaviveka
author: Maṇḍanamiśra
classification_notes: The Vibhramaviveka ('Discernment of Error') by Maṇḍanamiśra (ca. 2337 tokens) is a philosophical treatise on error theory (khyāti) within the Advaita Vedānta tradition. It is a specialist work in Indian epistemology, important for advanced study of Vedānta.

title: Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara
author: Mādhava
classification_notes: Mādhava's Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara is a work in the Mīmāṃsā tradition, systematically presenting the principles (nyāya) of Vedic exegesis as formulated by the school of Jaimini. It serves as a guide to the core doctrines of Mīmāṃsā, focusing on the interpretation of Vedic injunctions. Its audience is mainly among scholars of Indian philosophy and ritual theory.

title: Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara
author: Mādhava
classification_notes: The Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara, authored by Mādhava, is a scholarly commentary and exposition on the Mīmāṃsā philosophy of Vedic exegesis, specifically elaborating on the Jaiminīyanyāyamālā. Mādhava is renowned for his encyclopedic work, the Sarvadarśanasaṃgraha. This text is a sophisticated resource for specialists in Mīmāṃsā and the history of Vedic interpretation, consolidating complex hermeneutical debates.

title: Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistarabhūmikā
author: Mādhava
classification_notes: Mādhava's Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistarabhūmikā is a commentary (bhūmikā) on the Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistara, an exposition of Mīmāṃsā philosophy. The work systematizes the rules of Vedic exegesis and ritual interpretation (Mīmāṃsā) and is an important text in the tradition of Mīmāṃsā scholasticism. It has primarily been studied by specialists in Indian philosophy and ritual theory.

title: Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistarabhūmikā
author: Mādhava
classification_notes: Mādhava's Jaiminīyanyāyamālāvistarabhūmikā is a commentary on the Jaiminīyanyāyamālā, a work on Mīmāṃsā philosophy. It provides detailed explanations of Mīmāṃsā principles of Vedic interpretation and is a valuable resource for scholars of the Mīmāṃsā school.

title: Sarvadarśanasaṃgraha
author: Mādhavācārya
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Philosophy; dcs_subject=Darshana; dcs_time_slot=medieval; dcs_author=Mādhava; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Mādhavācārya’s Sarvadarśanasaṃgraha is a celebrated doxographical compendium surveying sixteen ‘viewpoints’ of Indian philosophy, culminating in Advaita Vedānta. Although brief (about 600 words in this redaction), the work’s influence as a pedagogical survey has been substantial, though its compressed form is a product of a particular extract or epitome.

title: Anuvyākhyāna
author: Mādhva
classification_notes: This is a second manuscript of Mādhva's Anuvyākhyāna, a foundational text of Dvaita Vedanta that systematically exegetes the Brahma-sūtras from a dualistic perspective. This version is longer, suggesting a more complete recension. As with the earlier entry, its importance is high within Dvaita scholarship but specialist in the global Sanskrit corpus.

title: Anuvyākhyāna
author: Mādhva
classification_notes: Mādhva's Anuvyākhyāna is a foundational text of the Dvaita Vedanta school, comprising a systematic exegesis of the Brahma-sūtras. Written in a terse, argumentative style, it defends dualistic metaphysics against Advaita and other interpretations. The work is of central importance within the specialist tradition of Dvaita Vedanta, though less widely studied in the broader Sanskrit philosophical corpus.

title: Mahābhāratatātparyanirṇaya
author: Mādhva
classification_notes: The Mahābhāratatātparyanirṇaya by Mādhvācārya is a landmark commentary from the Dvaita Vedānta perspective. It offers a systematic interpretation of the Mahābhārata according to Madhva's dualist theology. This substantial work is essential for understanding the Dvaita tradition's relationship with the epic, but its readership is largely confined to specialists in Vedānta philosophy and Mahābhārata studies.

title: Mahābhāratatātparyanirṇaya
author: Mādhva
classification_notes: Mādhva's Mahābhāratatātparyanirṇaya is a Dvaita Vedānta commentary on the Mahābhārata, interpreting the epic through the lens of Madhva's dualistic theology. It is a key text for understanding the Mahābhārata's philosophical and devotional significance in the Dvaita tradition.

title: Kṛṣṇāmṛtamahārṇava
author: Mādhva Ānandatīrtha
classification_notes: The Kṛṣṇāmṛtamahārṇava (The Great Ocean of the Nectar of Krishna) by Mādhva Ānandatīrtha is a devotional text from the Madhva tradition of Vedānta, extolling the divine qualities of Kṛṣṇa. This concise work of about 3,350 words is a stotra or hymn of praise, likely composed in the late medieval period. It is significant within the Dvaita Vedānta community's devotional literature.

title: Kṛṣṇāmṛtamahārṇava
author: Mādhva Ānandatīrtha
classification_notes: Mādhva Ānandatīrtha's Kṛṣṇāmṛtamahārṇava (The Great Ocean of the Nectar of Kṛṣṇa) is a devotional hymn (stotra) composed by the founder of the Dvaita Vedānta tradition. This work (3,369 words) is a fervent expression of bhakti (devotion) to Kṛṣṇa. Its specialized theological and devotional content places it in the specialist tier globally and within the religion discovery group, where it is a valued text for scholars of the Dvaita school and bhakti literature.

title: Śiśupālavadha
author: Māgha
classification_notes: The Śiśupālavadha by Māgha is a celebrated mahākāvya of Classical Sanskrit literature. This 31305-word epic poem narrates the slaying of King Śiśupāla by Kṛṣṇa at the court of Yudhiṣṭhira. Renowned for its extraordinary verbal artistry, complex syntax, and richness of descriptive passages (including the famous description of the four seasons), it is a canonical text and a staple of traditional Sanskrit education.

title: Śiśupālavadha
author: Māgha
classification_notes: The Śiśupālavadha (The Slaying of Śiśupāla) is one of the five great Sanskrit mahākāvyas, composed by the 7th-century poet Māgha. Renowned for its sophisticated poetic techniques, intricate metrical patterns, and verbal dexterity, it narrates the episode from the Mahābhārata in which Kṛṣṇa slays the arrogant King Śiśupāla. With over 20,000 words, it stands as a monumental work in the Sanskrit literary tradition, widely studied for its literary theory and exemplary kāvya style.

title: Mārkaṇḍeyapurāṇa
author: Mārkaṇḍeyapurāṇa1
classification_notes: The Mārkaṇḍeyapurāṇa is one of the eighteen Mahāpurāṇas, a significant medieval Sanskrit text blending mythology, cosmology, genealogical lore, and ethical teaching. It is especially famous for containing the Devī Māhātmya (Glorification of the Goddess), a foundational text for Śākta traditions. The work is well known across Indological curricula and is frequently cited in studies of purāṇic religion, female divinity, and Hindu theology.

title: Māṭharavṛtti
author: Māṭhara
classification_notes: Māṭharavṛtti is a commentary on Īśvarakṛṣṇa's Sāṃkhyakārikā, attributed to Māṭhara. It is an important early exegetical work in the Sāṃkhya tradition.

title: Māṭharavṛtti
author: Māṭhara
classification_notes: Māṭhara's Māṭharavṛtti is one of the earliest surviving commentaries on Īśvarakṛṣṇa's Sāṃkhyakārikā, the foundational text of the Sāṃkhya school. As a vṛtti (prose exposition), it provides a line-by-line explanation of the kārikās and is an important witness to the early interpretive tradition of Sāṃkhya philosophy. Its preservation is fragmentary in some parts, but it remains a key source for reconstructing classical Sāṃkhya doctrine.

title: Citralakṣaṇam
author: Nagnajit
classification_notes: The Citralakṣaṇam by Nagnajit is a Sanskrit technical treatise on the theory and practice of painting, belonging to the genre of citralakṣaṇa (iconometric and aesthetic guidelines for visual art). Its 4,573 tokens provide instructions on proportions, color, and composition. While specialist even within the broader scope of Sanskrit technical literature, it is an early and important source for the history of Indian art theory.

title: Citralakṣaṇam
author: Nagnajit
classification_notes: The Citralakṣaṇam by Nagnajit is a technical treatise on the art of painting (citra). It is one of the few surviving Sanskrit texts on aesthetics and visual art, providing rules for composition, proportion, and color. This work is a valuable resource for specialists in Indian art history, aesthetics, and the technical literature of classical India.

title: Bodhasāra
author: Narahari
classification_notes: Narahari's Bodhasāra is a Jain didactic work written in Sanskrit, likely from the Early Modern period, focusing on the acquisition of spiritual knowledge (bodha) and its practical application for liberation. The text presents a synthesis of Jain ethical, metaphysical, and soteriological teachings in accessible verse. It holds a specialist status both within the broader Sanskrit corpus and within the discovery group for Jain literature, where it is one of many post-classical devotional and instructional works that inform scholars of Jain lay and monastic practice.

title: Bodhasāra
author: Narahari
classification_notes: Narahari's Bodhasāra is a medieval philosophical text of uncertain affiliation, possibly synthesizing Buddhist or Jain doctrines. Its title suggests it is a compendium of wisdom. However, with limited contextual metadata, it ranks as an obscure work in the Sanskrit corpus, known only to a few specialists.

title: Dharmasaṃgraha
author: Nāgārjuna
classification_notes: Dharmasaṃgraha is a concise Buddhist doctrinal text attributed to the philosopher Nāgārjuna. It enumerates key concepts of Buddhist thought, serving as a digest of essential teachings. While not as widely studied as Nāgārjuna's philosophical works, it remains of interest in Buddhist studies.

title: Dharmasaṃgraha
author: Nāgārjuna
classification_notes: The Dharmasaṃgraha attributed to Nāgārjuna is a 2,671-word Buddhist doxographical and lexical text compiling lists of key technical terms (dharmas) from the Mahāyāna tradition. It functions as a concise manual of doctrine, covering topics such as the perfections, powers, and paths. It is a valuable reference for scholars of Buddhist philosophy and textual traditions.

title: Mūlamadhyamakārikāḥ
author: Nāgārjuna
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Buddhist Scripture; dcs_subject=Buddhist; dcs_time_slot=epic; dcs_author=Nāgārjuna; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Mūlamadhyamakārikāḥ is the magnum opus of Nāgārjuna, the foundational text of the Madhyamaka school of Buddhist philosophy. It is one of the most studied and commented-upon works in the entire Buddhist canon.

title: Ratnāvalī
author: Nāgārjuna
classification_notes: The Ratnāvalī (The Precious Garland) by Nāgārjuna is a 3,325-word philosophical and ethical treatise in the form of a letter to a king. It presents Mahāyāna Buddhist teachings on the bodhisattva path, emptiness, and worldly ethics in an accessible verse format. It is one of Nāgārjuna's most widely studied works, valued for its breadth and clarity within Buddhist traditions and by scholars of Indian philosophy.

title: Ratnāvalī
author: Nāgārjuna
classification_notes: Nāgārjuna's Ratnāvalī is a concise and luminous exposition of Madhyamaka philosophy in verse, addressed to a king (Sātavāhana). It teaches the emptiness of all phenomena, dependent origination, and the bodhisattva path. As a foundational work of Mahāyāna Buddhism, it is widely read and commented upon in both Indian and Tibetan traditions.

title: Sekoddeśa
author: Nāropa
classification_notes: Nāropa's Sekoddeśa is a short but highly significant Buddhist tantric commentary that presents the esoteric meaning of the Kālacakra initiation. Attributed to the great Indian master Nāropa, it is a key text in the Kālacakra tradition and is studied primarily within Indo-Tibetan Buddhist tantric scholarship.

title: Sekoddeśa
author: Nāropa
classification_notes: Nāropa’s Sekoddeśa is a concise tantric work explaining the initiation (sekha) in the Kālacakra tradition. It is a key source for later Tibetan Buddhist exegesis.

title: Hitopadeśa
author: Nārāyaṇa
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Narrative Literature; dcs_subject=Katha; dcs_time_slot=medieval; dcs_author=Nārāyaṇa; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Nārāyaṇa's Hitopadeśa is a celebrated collection of fables and moral stories. It is widely read in both scholarly and general contexts, and is often used as a teaching text for Sanskrit.

title: Nārāyaṇīya
author: Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa
classification_notes: Nārāyaṇīya is a celebrated devotional kāvya by Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa, composed in 1587 CE. It condenses the Bhāgavata Purāṇa in 1000 verses, extolling the deeds of Lord Kṛṣṇa. It is especially revered in Kerala for its poetic elegance and depth of bhakti.

title: Nārāyaṇīya
author: Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa
classification_notes: The Nārāyaṇīya by Nārāyaṇabhaṭṭa is a celebrated devotional poem (stotra) in praise of Lord Kṛṣṇa, composed in the Malayalam region of South India. It is a condensed retelling of the Bhāgavata Purāṇa in 1034 verses and is a beloved text in the Kṛṣṇa bhakti tradition. Its literary grace and devotional fervor have made it widely popular among devotees and scholars of Hindu devotional literature.

title: Kaliviḍambana
author: Nīlakaṇṭhadīkṣita
classification_notes: The Kaliviḍambana (The Mockery of the Kali Age) by Nīlakaṇṭhadīkṣita is a satirical poem that humorously criticizes the moral and social decline of the Kali Yuga. It is a fine example of late Sanskrit kāvya with a sharp wit. While not as widely read as his other works like the Śivalīlārṇava, it is appreciated by connoisseurs of Sanskrit satire and late classical poetry.

title: Kaliviḍambana
author: Nīlakaṇṭhadīkṣita
classification_notes: Nīlakaṇṭhadīkṣita's Kaliviḍambana is a satirical poem that lampoons the vices of the Kali age. Written in elegant and witty Sanskrit verse, it critiques hypocrisy, greed, and moral decline in society. The work is valued by specialists in later kāvya literature and social history.

title: Navasāhasāṅkacarita
author: Padmagupta
classification_notes: Padmagupta’s Navasāhasāṅkacarita (28,110 words) is a historical courtly epic in Sanskrit celebrating the Paramāra king Sindhurāja. It is an important example of the late mahākāvya genre and is of interest to specialists in Sanskrit court poetry.

title: Navasāhasāṅkacarita
author: Padmagupta
classification_notes: Padmagupta's Navasāhasāṅkacarita is a Sanskrit mahākāvya (courtly epic) recounting the legendary exploits of King Navasāhasāṅka, widely identified with the Paramāra ruler Sindhurāja. Composed in the ornate style of the tenth century, the poem draws on the kāvya and itihāsa traditions to celebrate dynastic achievement through embedded narratives. While significant within the corpus of medieval Sanskrit courtly literature, its specialist popularity reflects limited readership beyond Indological scholarship on Paramāra historiography and classical metrical romance.

title: Yogasūtra
author: Patañjali
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Philosophy; dcs_subject=Yoga; dcs_time_slot=epic; dcs_author=Patañjali; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Yogasūtra of Patañjali is the foundational text of the Yoga darśana, systematizing the theory and practice of yoga in four pādas (chapters). It defines the eight-limbed path (aṣṭāṅga-yoga) and is arguably the most widely known work of Indian philosophy globally, studied across disciplines from Indology to modern yoga practice. Despite its brevity, its influence on both classical Indian thought and global spirituality is immense.

title: Pauṣkarasaṃhitā
author: Pauṣkarasaṃhitā
classification_notes: The Pauṣkarasaṃhitā, attributed to the Pāñcarātra tradition, is a Śaiva-Śākta tantric saṃhitā combining ritual procedure, mantraśāstra, and theology. Its length (over 53,000 words) indicates a substantial manual; it is primarily utilized in specialist research on Tantric and Śaiva traditions.

title: Avayavinirākaraṇa
author: Paṇḍitāśoka
classification_notes: Paṇḍitāśoka's Avayavinirākaraṇa is a polemical Nyāya work that argues against the reality of wholes (avayavī), engaging in the ongoing debate between atomist and whole-advocating schools of Indian logic. It is an important source for understanding Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika metaphysics.

title: Avayavinirākaraṇa
author: Paṇḍitāśoka
classification_notes: Avayavinirākaraṇa is a Buddhist philosophical text by Paṇḍitāśoka, arguing against the concept of a whole (avayavin) as an entity distinct from its parts. It represents late Indian Buddhist logic.

title: Sāmānyadūṣaṇadikprasāritā
author: Paṇḍitāśoka
classification_notes: Classification: The 'Sāmānyadūṣaṇadikprasāritā' is a scholar-initiated Buddhist philosophical polemic verse work by Paṇḍitāśoka. It is considered a late medieval Indian Buddhist Sanskrit text. As a specialist bellatorwork within Buddhist logico-epistemological tradition, it is relatively obscure globally compared to other Buddhist texts but within its niche philosophy category it has some recognition.

title: Sāmānyadūṣaṇadikprasāritā
author: Paṇḍitāśoka
classification_notes: The Sāmānyadūṣaṇadikprasāritā (Refutation of the General [Definition] Extended in All Directions) by Paṇḍitāśoka is a short philosophical treatise (about 778 words) engaging with Nyāya logic, likely a polemical work challenging specific definitions. It is a very obscure text, known to a handful of specialists in later medieval Nyāya philosophy and manuscript studies.

title: Caitanyacandrāmṛta
author: Prabodhānandasarasvatī
classification_notes: Prabodhānandasarasvatī's Caitanyacandrāmṛta is a Sanskrit stotra in praise of the Bengali saint Caitanya, composed from a Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava perspective. The poem uses ornate kāvya style to celebrate Caitanya's life and teachings. Its readership is largely confined to specialists in Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava literature and early modern Sanskrit devotional poetry.

title: Caitanyacandrāmṛta
author: Prabodhānandasarasvatī
classification_notes: Prabodhānandasarasvatī's Caitanyacandrāmṛta is a devotional hymn (stotra) in praise of Caitanya Mahāprabhu, composed in an early modern period. It is an obscure work, mainly of interest to specialists in Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava devotional literature.

title: Prātimokṣasūtra
author: Prātimokṣasūtra
classification_notes: The Prātimokṣasūtra (7,363 words) of the Mūlasarvāstivādins is a foundational Buddhist monastic code detailing the rules of conduct for monks and nuns. It is an essential but specialist source for the study of Buddhist monastic discipline.

title: Rājataraṅginī
author: Pseudo-Jonarāja Jonarāja
classification_notes: This continuation of Kalhaṇa's Rājataraṅginī by Pseudo-Jonarāja records the later history of Kashmir. Though of disputed authorship, it is a valuable source for medieval Kashmiri political history.

title: Śāstradīpikā
author: Pārthasārathimiśra
classification_notes: The Śāstradīpikā by Pārthasārathimiśra is a 34503-word commentary on the Mīmāṃsā Sūtras of Jaimini. This medieval work is a lucid and systematic exposition of the principles of Vedic exegesis, ritual interpretation, and the philosophy of dharma. It is a standard textbook in the Mīmāṃsā tradition and a key resource for scholars of Indian hermeneutics.

title: Śāstradīpikā
author: Pārthasārathimiśra
classification_notes: The Śāstradīpikā ('Lamp on the Teachings') by Pārthasārathimiśra is a major commentary on the Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā Sūtras, particularly the Śābara Bhāṣya. It is a substantial and highly respected work within the Mīmāṃsā tradition, known for its clear exposition and rigorous argumentation. Its length (c. 19,000 words) is typical of a major commentary. However, its readership is almost entirely limited to specialists in Mīmāṃsā and Indian philosophy.

title: Aṣṭādhyāyī
author: Pāṇini
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Grammar; dcs_subject=Paniniya; dcs_time_slot=epic; dcs_author=Pāṇini; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Pāṇini's Aṣṭādhyāyī is the foundational text of Sanskrit grammar and one of the most remarkable intellectual achievements of ancient India. Composed as a set of approximately 4,000 sūtras, it provides a complete and generative description of Sanskrit phonology, morphology, and syntax. Its influence extends far beyond linguistics to the very structure of Sanskrit intellectual traditions. The Aṣṭādhyāyī is a canonical work in the global study of language and is essential for any serious engagement with Sanskrit.

title: Muktācarita
author: Raghunāthadāsagosvāmin
classification_notes: Muktācarita is a narrative poem by the Gaudiya Vaishnava theologian Raghunāthadāsagosvāmin. The work celebrates the life of the female saint Muktādevī, blending kavya style with bhakti devotion. It is of specialist interest both within the broader corpus and within devotional kavya.

title: Muktācarita
author: Raghunāthadāsagosvāmin
classification_notes: Muktācarita is a poetic hagiography composed by the Gaudiya saint Raghunāthadāsagosvāmin, recounting the life of Krishna Chaitanya. The work is written in Sanskrit kavya style and is an important text within the Gaudiya Vaishnava tradition, though less widely known outside specialist circles.

title: Apohasiddhi
author: Ratnakīrti
classification_notes: The Apohasiddhi ('Establishment of Exclusion') by Ratnakīrti is a seminal text in Buddhist logic and epistemology. It defends the theory of apoha (exclusion), which holds that words refer to concepts by excluding other possibilities, a key doctrine of the Buddhist logico-epistemological tradition (pramāṇa). This work is essential reading for specialists in Buddhist philosophy and the history of Indian logic.

title: Apohasiddhi
author: Ratnakīrti
classification_notes: Ratnakīrti's Apohasiddhi ('Proof of Exclusion') is a treatise on Buddhist semantics and logic, defending the theory of apoha (exclusion) as the meaning of words. It is a key text in the Buddhist epistemological tradition.

title: Sāratama
author: Ratnākaraśānti
classification_notes: The Sāratama by Ratnākaraśānti is a Buddhist philosophical work, likely a commentary or treatise within the Abhidharma or Yogācāra tradition. Ratnākaraśānti was a prominent scholar at the Vikramaśīla monastery and a key figure in late Indian Buddhist scholasticism. This text is of significant interest to specialists in Buddhist philosophy and the history of Indian logic and epistemology.

title: Sāratama
author: Ratnākaraśānti
classification_notes: Ratnākaraśānti’s Sāratama is an extensive (42,665-word) Buddhist philosophical commentary in the Yogācāra tradition. Its exegetical depth makes it an important but specialist resource for scholars of late Indian Buddhist thought.

title: Rākṣasakāvya
author: Ravideva
classification_notes: The Rākṣasakāvya by Ravideva is a medieval Sanskrit kāvya poem narrating the exploits of a rākṣasa (demon). It is an example of later classical poetic composition in the ornate kāvya style, likely composed in the 13th–15th centuries. The work is known primarily to specialists in medieval Sanskrit literature and kāvya traditions.

title: Rākṣasakāvya
author: Ravideva
classification_notes: Classification: The 'Rākṣasakāvya' of Ravideva is a minor Sanskrit ornate poem (kavya) of very limited length (617 words). It is obscure in all comparison sets because of its brevity, lack of commentary tradition, and narrow manuscript circulation.

title: Rākṣasakāvyaṭīkā
author: Ravideva
classification_notes: The Rākṣasakāvyaṭīkā, by Ravideva, is a commentary (ṭīkā) on the Rākṣasakāvya, a work of Sanskrit poetry. As a specialized exegetical text, it belongs to the tradition of Alamkāraśāstra and literary criticism. This commentary is of value to scholars of Sanskrit poetics and the history of literary interpretation, but its niche scope means it is consulted only by specialists in the field.

title: Rākṣasakāvyaṭīkā
author: Ravideva
classification_notes: Ravideva's Rākṣasakāvyaṭīkā is a commentary on a lost or obscure kāvya, the Rākṣasakāvya. This short gloss (ca. 2300 words) provides insight into the commentarial tradition surrounding Sanskrit literary works and is primarily of interest to scholars of Sanskrit poetics (alaṃkāraśāstra) and literary criticism.

title: Śṛṅgāratilaka
author: Rudrabhaṭṭaḥ
classification_notes: Rudrabhaṭṭa's Śṛṅgāratilaka is a lyrical poem celebrating erotic love (śṛṅgāra). It exemplifies the kāvya tradition with its ornate style and vivid descriptions of amorous scenes.

title: Śṛṅgāratilaka
author: Rudrabhaṭṭaḥ
classification_notes: Rudrabhaṭṭa's Śṛṅgāratilaka is a poem in the Sanskrit kāvya tradition, focusing on the aesthetic of love (śṛṅgāra rasa). Though not among the most celebrated court epics, it is regarded as a competent example of medieval ornate poetry and is of moderate interest within the field of kāvya studies. Within the broader Sanskrit corpus it falls into the specialist tier.

title: Sahṛdayalīlā
author: Ruyyaka
classification_notes: The Sahṛdayalīlā by Ruyyaka is a work on Sanskrit poetics (alaṃkāraśāstra), likely discussing figures of speech and literary aesthetics. It is a specialist work within the alaṃkāra tradition.

title: Sahṛdayalīlā
author: Ruyyaka
classification_notes: Ruyyaka's Sahṛdayalīlā is a concise treatise on poetic aesthetics, focusing on the concept of rasa (aesthetic enjoyment) and the qualities of a sensitive reader (sahṛdaya). It belongs to the tradition of Sanskrit poetics (alaṃkāraśāstra) and is valued for its theoretical insights.

title: Sādhanadīpikā
author: Rādhākṛṣṇadāsagosvāmin
classification_notes: Classification: The 'Sādhanadīpikā' is a later bhakti manual of the Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava tradition written by Rādhākṛṣṇadāsagosvāmin. It is a specialist devotional work, significant for worshippers of the Caitanya tradition but relatively obscure in the Sanskrit canon; within the Stotra genre it ranks as specialist due to its moderate length and niche audience.

title: Sādhanadīpikā
author: Rādhākṛṣṇadāsagosvāmin
classification_notes: The Sādhanadīpikā by Rādhākṛṣṇadāsagosvāmin is a 30495-word early modern Sanskrit manual on the practice (sādhana) of Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava devotionalism. It provides detailed instructions on ritual, mantra, and meditation techniques. This work is a specialist guide within the Gauḍīya tradition, studied by scholars of bhakti and the history of Vaiṣṇava religiosity.

title: Mahāgaṇapatistotra
author: Rāghavacaitanya
classification_notes: Rāghavacaitanya's Mahāgaṇapatistotra is a devotional hymn in praise of Lord Gaṇapati, composed in the stotra tradition. It is recited for removing obstacles and is part of the broader bhakti literature dedicated to Gaṇeśa.

title: Mahāgaṇapatistotra
author: Rāghavacaitanya
classification_notes: The Mahāgaṇapatistotra is a devotional hymn (stotra) dedicated to Gaṇapati, composed by Rāghavacaitanya. While such stotras are numerous in the bhakti tradition, this particular hymn is known primarily through specialized collections and is not as widely circulated as major stotras like the Gaṇeśa Atharvaśīrṣa.

title: Mahāgaṇapatistotrabhāṣya
author: Rāghavacaitanya
classification_notes: This work is Rāghavacaitanya's own commentary (bhāṣya) on his Mahāgaṇapatistotra, providing explanatory notes on the mantra-like hymn. It offers insight into the ritual and theological significance of the stotra.

title: Mahāgaṇapatistotrabhāṣya
author: Rāghavacaitanya
classification_notes: The Mahāgaṇapatistotrabhāṣya by Rāghavacaitanya is the commentary (bhāṣya) on a hymn to Mahāgaṇapati. As a late and narrow devotional commentary, it circulates among specialists in Vaiṣṇava or Gauḍīya stotra tradition.

title: Bhagavadgītābhāṣya
author: Rāmānuja
classification_notes: The Bhagavadgītābhāṣya by Rāmānuja is a major Viśiṣṭādvaita commentary on the Bhagavad Gītā. Composed by the great 12th-century theologian, it presents a theistic, qualified non-dual interpretation that has deeply influenced later Vaiṣṇava philosophy and practice. The text is central to Vedānta studies and is widely read by students of Indian philosophy, comparative theology, and Rāmānuja's thought.

title: Bhagavadgītābhāṣya
author: Rāmānuja
classification_notes: The Bhagavadgītābhāṣya by Rāmānuja (ca. 44,547 tokens) is a foundational commentary on the Bhagavadgītā from the Viśiṣṭādvaita Vedānta perspective. It is a major theological work widely studied in Indian philosophy and religious studies.

title: Vedārthasaṃgraha
author: Rāmānuja
classification_notes: The Vedārthasaṃgraha (Compendium of the Meaning of the Vedas) is a seminal theological work by Rāmānuja, the foundational theologian of Viśiṣṭādvaita Vedānta. In approximately 19,200 tokens, Rāmānuja presents his interpretation of Upaniṣadic texts, arguing for a qualified non-dualism against Advaita. The work is major within Vedānta and commands a common standing across Sanskrit philosophical literature, being central to the study of medieval Indian theology.

title: Vedārthasaṃgraha
author: Rāmānuja
classification_notes: The Vedārthasaṃgraha by Rāmānuja (ca. 13,286 tokens) is a seminal work of Viśiṣṭādvaita Vedānta, summarizing the meaning of the Vedas as interpreted in the tradition. It is a major philosophical text widely studied within Vedānta and Indian philosophy, with common readership across the broader tradition.

title: Haribhaktirasāmṛtasindhu
author: Rūpagosvāmin
classification_notes: Rūpagosvāmin's Haribhaktirasāmṛtasindhu (The Ocean of the Nectar of Devotion to Hari) is a foundational theological text for the Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava tradition, systematizing bhakti-rasa. It combines devotional theology with poetic theory and is highly influential within the Caitanya tradition. In the overall Sanskrit corpus it is a common-tier work, but within bhakti and devotional literature it ranks as major.

title: Haribhaktirasāmṛtasindhu
author: Rūpagosvāmin
classification_notes: Rūpagosvāmin’s Haribhaktirasāmṛtasindhu is the definitive theological treatise on the aesthetics of bhakti from the Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava perspective. It systematizes the theory of devotional rasa and has been hugely influential in the Caitanya tradition.

title: Laghubhāgavatāmṛta
author: Rūpagosvāmin
classification_notes: Rūpagosvāmin's Laghubhāgavatāmṛta is a devotional treatise within the Gauḍīya Vaiṣṇava tradition, summarizing the essential teachings on the nature, forms, and pastimes of Kṛṣṇa. It serves as a concise compendium for devotees, emphasizing bhakti (devotion) and the theological framework established by Caitanya. The work is significant for understanding medieval Vaiṣṇava theology in Bengal.

title: Laghubhāgavatāmṛta
author: Rūpagosvāmin
classification_notes: The Laghubhāgavatāmṛta is a theological treatise by the Gaudiya Vaishnava saint Rūpagosvāmin, summarizing the essence of the Bhāgavatapurāṇa in a concise form. It is of specialist interest within the broader tradition and within the Gaudiya devotional corpus.

title: Āptamīmāṃsā
author: Samantabhadra
classification_notes: Samantabhadra's Āptamīmāṃsā (Investigation of the Authoritative) is a foundational Jain philosophical text arguing for the validity of the Jain scriptures through a logical framework akin to the nyāya-influenced pramāṇa theory. It is a major text within Jain philosophy and is widely cited in studies of non-Buddhist Indian logic. In the broader Sanskrit corpus it is specialist-tier, but within Jain literature it is a common reference.

title: Āptamīmāṃsā
author: Samantabhadra
classification_notes: The Āptamīmāṃsā ('Investigation of the Authoritative') by Samantabhadra is a foundational text of Jain logic and epistemology. It defends the validity of the Jain scriptures and the omniscience of the Jinas through rigorous philosophical argument. This work is a key source for the study of Jain philosophy and the history of Indian logic, particularly within the Digambara tradition.

title: Kathāsaritsāgara
author: Somadeva
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Narrative Literature; dcs_subject=Katha; dcs_time_slot=medieval; dcs_author=Somadeva; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Kathāsaritsāgara (Ocean of the Streams of Stories) by Somadeva is arguably the greatest collection of Sanskrit narrative tales, composed in Kashmir. It is a poetic retelling of the earlier Bṛhatkathā, presenting a vast compendium of frame stories and nested fables. As one of the most accessible and celebrated works of Sanskrit narrative, it has a broad readership among students of Indian literature and folklore, though its sheer length and genre placement keep it from being as canonical as epic or drama.

title: Triṃśikāvijñaptibhāṣya
author: Sthiramati
classification_notes: Sthiramati's Triṃśikāvijñaptibhāṣya is a key Yogacara commentary on Vasubandhu's Triṃśikā, explicating the doctrine of consciousness-only (vijñapti-mātra). It is an essential text for advanced study of Buddhist idealist philosophy.

title: Triṃśikāvijñaptibhāṣya
author: Sthiramati
classification_notes: Sthiramati's Triṃśikāvijñaptibhāṣya is a commentary on Vasubandhu's Triṃśikā, a foundational Yogācāra text. It elucidates the doctrine of consciousness-only (vijñaptimātra) and is essential for the study of Buddhist idealist philosophy.

title: Vāsavadattā
author: Subandhu
classification_notes: Subandhu's Vāsavadattā is a celebrated Sanskrit prose romance (kathā) and a classic of the kāvya tradition. The work narrates the love story of Prince Kandarpaketu and Princess Vāsavadattā, characterized by ornate descriptions, intricate wordplay, and emotional depth. As a pioneering work of Sanskrit literary prose, it has been widely studied and praised for its stylistic virtuosity.

title: Vāsavadattā
author: Subandhu
classification_notes: Subandhu's Vāsavadattā is a celebrated Sanskrit prose romance (kathā), a landmark in the development of Sanskrit literary prose (gadya-kāvya). It tells the story of the love between Prince Kandarpaketu and Princess Vāsavadattā, and is noted for its ornate style and complex wordplay. The work is widely studied in the context of classical Sanskrit literature and is of common interest to scholars of kāvya. Its length (5369 words) is moderate for a prose romance.

title: Mīmāṃsāślokavārttikakāśikā
author: Sucaritamiśra
classification_notes: Classification: The 'Mīmāṃsāślokavārttikakāśikā' of Sucaritamiśra is a detailed commentary on the Ślokavārttika of Kumārila Bhaṭṭa, a central text in the Mīmāṃsā philosophical tradition. It is generally specialist, but within the Mīmāṃsā fraternity it is relatively well-known—a specialist at the upper end.

title: Mīmāṃsāślokavārttikakāśikā
author: Sucaritamiśra
classification_notes: Sucaritamiśra's Mīmāṃsāślokavārttikakāśikā is a commentary on the Ślokavārttika of Kumārila Bhaṭṭa, a foundational text of the Mīmāṃsā school. This subcommentary (kāśikā) is a dense scholastic work that engages in detailed exegesis of Kumārila's arguments on Vedic interpretation. It is a specialist text essential for advanced study of Mīmāṃsā.

title: Sukavihṛdayānandinī
author: Sulhaṇa
classification_notes: The Sukavihṛdayānandinī by Sulhaṇa is a commentary on the Vṛttaratnākara, a classical Sanskrit prosody manual by Kedāra Bhaṭṭa. This commentary is a specialized scholastic work within the Sanskrit grammatical and metrical tradition, consulted by experts in metrics (chandas) and the history of Sanskrit poetics.

title: Sukavihṛdayānandinī
author: Sulhaṇa
classification_notes: Sulhaṇa's Sukavihṛdayānandinī is a commentary on Kedāra Bhaṭṭa's Vr̥ttaratnākara, a standard work on Sanskrit metrics (chandas). This commentary elucidates the metrical rules with examples and is of interest primarily to specialists in Sanskrit prosody and medieval scholastic commentary traditions.

title: Suśrutasaṃhitā
author: Suśruta
classification_notes: The Suśrutasaṃhitā is a foundational text of Ayurvedic medicine, attributed to the legendary surgeon Suśruta. It covers surgery, anatomy, pathology, and pharmacology, and is one of the two great ancient Indian medical treatises (the other being the Carakasaṃhitā). It is widely studied in the history of medicine and Indian science, occupying a common position in the global Sanskrit corpus.

title: Haṭhayogapradīpikā
author: Svātmārāma
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Philosophy; dcs_subject=Yoga; dcs_time_slot=late; dcs_author=Svātmarāma; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Svātmārāma’s Haṭhayogapradīpikā is a seminal technical treatise systematizing haṭha yoga practice, bridging earlier tantric and ascetic traditions with later yogic orthodoxy. Its concision (about 4,300 words) belies its profound influence on modern global yoga. The work remains a core text in both scholarly curricula and practitioner study.

title: Kokilasaṃdeśa
author: Uddaṇḍa Śāstrī
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Kavya; dcs_time_slot=late; dcs_author=Uddaṇḍa; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Uddaṇḍa Śāstrī’s Kokilasaṃdeśa is a late Sanskrit poetic work (2,652 words) in the sandeśa-kāvya (messenger poem) genre. While it has a place in the regional literary history of Kerala, it remains a specialist text within Sanskrit belles-lettres.

title: AB-mt
author: Unknown
classification_notes: This entry likely represents the Aitareya Brāhmaṇa, a Vedic prose text explaining the rituals of the Rigveda. The abbreviation 'AB' and word count support this identification, though the source is a modern translation.

title: AU-Olivelle
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Aitareya Upaniṣad (recorded here as 'AU-Olivelle') is a principal Upaniṣad belonging to the Ṛgveda. It contains a famous creation myth and discussions on the nature of the self. It is a major Upaniṣad, widely read in courses on Indian philosophy and Hinduism, and frequently cited in scholarly works.

title: Abhidharmasamuccayabhāṣya
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Abhidharmasamuccayabhāṣya is a commentary on Asaṅga's Abhidharmasamuccaya, a key Yogācāra text. Typically attributed to Sthiramati or a related commentator, it expands on the Abhidharma categories from a Mahāyāna perspective. This text of about 39,300 words is essential for specialists studying Yogācāra scholasticism and Indian Buddhist commentarial literature.

title: Abhidharmasamuccayabhāṣya
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Abhidharmasamuccayabhāṣya is a Sanskrit commentary on the Abhidharmasamuccaya, a key Yogacara text. Attributed in tradition to Asaṅga but of uncertain authorship, this work is vital for specialists in Buddhist Abhidharma and Yogacara philosophy. Its technical subject matter and incomplete preservation restrict its readership to a specialist audience.

title: Abhinavacintāmaṇi
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Abhinavacintāmaṇi, attributed to Cakrapāṇidāsa, is a treatise on rasashastra (alchemy and iatrochemistry). It presents methods for preparing medicinal minerals and metals, reflecting the intersection of medicine and alchemy in medieval India.

title: Abhisamayālaṃkāra
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Abhisamayālaṃkāra (Ornament of Realization), traditionally attributed to Maitreya through Asaṅga, is a key Mahāyāna Buddhist philosophical treatise outlining the stages of the bodhisattva path and the realization of emptiness. This 4,227-word text is a core work in the Prajñāpāramitā commentarial tradition and remains widely studied in Tibetan and East Asian Buddhism. Its systematic presentation of the hidden meaning of the Prajñāpāramitā sūtras gives it canonical status within Buddhist scholasticism.

title: Abhisamayālaṃkāra
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Abhisamayālaṃkāra (Ornament of Clear Realization) is a key Mahayana Buddhist text, commentarial on the Prajñāpāramitā sutras and attributed to Maitreyanātha/Asaṅga. It is a central work in Tibetan Buddhist scholasticism for the study of the stages of the path. It is well-known among Buddhist studies scholars.

title: Acintyastava
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Buddhist Scripture; dcs_subject=Buddhist; dcs_time_slot=medieval; dcs_author=Nāgārjuna; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Acintyastava, attributed to Nāgārjuna, is a short Buddhist hymn. It is known within Buddhist philosophical circles but has limited reach beyond specialists.

title: Advayaśatikā prajñāpāramitā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: Classification: The 'Advayaśatikā prajñāpāramitā' is a very short Mahāyāna Buddhist sūtra belonging to the Prajñāpāramitā class. It is a specialist text—mainly of interest to scholars of late Indian Buddhist sūtra literature—and it is also specialist within its own group due to its brevity and lower circulation.

title: Agastīyaratnaparīkṣā
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Technical Literature; dcs_subject=Ratnashastra; dcs_time_slot=late; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Agastīyaratnaparīkṣā is a short technical treatise on gemology (ratnaśāstra), attributed to the sage Agastya. It classifies precious stones, describes their properties, tests, and uses. As a niche work within the medieval technical literature on jewels, it is of interest primarily to historians of ancient materials and craft knowledge.

title: Agnipurāṇa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Agnipurāṇa is a classical Sanskrit Purāṇa, traditionally ascribed to the sage Vyāsa but of composite authorship. With ca. 6250 tokens in the present extract, it forms a key encyclopedia of mythology, ritual, and dharma. It is commonly read among students of Purāṇic literature, though its fragmentary representation here limits scope.

title: Agnipurāṇa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Agnipurāṇa is one of the major eighteen Purāṇas, attributed to the sage Agni. It is an encyclopedic work covering cosmology, mythology, ritual, law, medicine, and art. It holds a central place in Puranic literature and Hindu tradition.

title: Agnipurāṇa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Agnipurāṇa is one of the eighteen major Purāṇas, a vast encyclopedic work (c. 179,700 words) covering mythology, ritual, cosmology, politics, medicine, art, and many other topics. As a Purāṇa it is a core text of the Hindu tradition, but its enormous scope and the existence of more popular Purāṇas (like the Bhāgavata) mean it is less frequently read in its entirety. Within the Sanskrit corpus, it occupies a common tier, being consulted by scholars and advanced students across multiple fields.

title: Ahoratravratakathā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Ahoratravratakathā is an anonymous narrative describing the observance and merits of a day-and-night vow (ahoratra vrata). It belongs to the genre of vratakathā, popular in medieval Puranic and devotional literature, serving both ritual instruction and edification.

title: Ahoratravratakathā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Ahoratravratakathā is a 2158-word medieval Sanskrit narrative recounting the story and ritual of the day-and-night vow (ahoratravrata), dedicated to a deity (likely Śiva or Viṣṇu). This anonymous text belongs to the genre of vrata kathā (vow narratives) found in purāṇic and folk religious literature. It is a specialist source for the study of popular Hindu religious practices and vow traditions.

title: Ahorātravratacaityasevānuśaṃsāvadāna
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Ahorātravratacaityasevānuśaṃsāvadāna is a Buddhist avadāna text, recounting the benefits of performing the day-and-night vow at a caitya. This short text (about 6,200 words) belongs to the genre of narrative literature that promotes meritorious deeds (avadānas). It is a relatively obscure work, known chiefly to specialists in Buddhist narrative and Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya literature.

title: Aitareyabrāhmaṇa

title: Aitareyāraṇyaka

title: Amaraughaśāsana
author: Unknown

author: Unknown

title: Amṛtabindūpaniṣat
author: Unknown

title: Anityatāsūtra
author: Unknown

title: Antarvyāptisamarthana
author: Unknown

title: Antarvyāptisamarthana
author: Unknown

title: Arthaviniścayasūtra
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Arthaviniścayasūtra is a relatively brief Mahāyāna Buddhist sūtra in Sanskrit, dealing with the definitive exposition of key doctrinal terms. Within the vast corpus of Sanskrit Buddhist sūtras, it occupies a niche position, being of interest primarily to scholars of Mahāyāna doctrinal literature rather than to a broad readership.

title: Arthaviniścayasūtra

As a core Vedic text, it is a major work within the Vedic Texts scope and a common work globally, widely read by students and scholars of Indology, religious studies, and ancient history.

title: Avadānaśataka
author: Unknown

title: Ayurvedarasāyana
author: Unknown

title: AĀ-mt

It sits at a common tier within the broader Sanskrit corpus.

title: Aśokāvadāna
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Aśokāvadāna is a cycle of Buddhist legends centering on King Aśoka and his interactions with the monastic community (saṃgha). The text belongs to the avadāna genre of exemplary narratives illustrating karmic retribution and the power of faith. It is a significant source for the history of Buddhism in the Mauryan and post-Mauryan periods and for the study of Buddhist narrative literature in Sanskrit. Within the Buddhist scripture scope, it holds a common specialist niche, while across the entire Sanskrit corpus its popularity is specialist.

title: Aśokāvadāna
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Aśokāvadāna is a Buddhist narrative text recounting the life and deeds of Emperor Aśoka and the early history of Buddhism. title: Aṣṭasāhasrikā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā Sūtra is one of the earliest and most important Mahāyāna Buddhist sūtras, foundational for the Prajñāpāramitā literature. It expounds the doctrine of emptiness (śūnyatā) and the perfection of wisdom. This text is widely studied in Buddhist studies and is a major work within the Mahāyāna scriptural tradition. Its length (29772 words) is substantial, representing a significant portion of the sūtra.

title: Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Aṣṭasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā (Perfection of Wisdom in 8,000 Lines) is one of the earliest and most influential Mahāyāna sūtras, foundational to the Prajñāpāramitā genre. It expounds the concept of emptiness (śūnyatā) and the bodhisattva path. Widely studied in Buddhist studies and comparative religion, it is a major text with a significant commentarial tradition and global scholarly attention.

title: Aṣṭāvakragīta
author: Unknown

The text teaches the path to liberation through the realization of the self's identity with Brahman. While traditionally influential in Vedantic circles, its authorship and precise dating remain uncertain. It is well-known among students of Indian spirituality and philosophy.

Its use is largely confined to specialists in Ayurveda and Sanskrit lexicography.

title: BaudhDhS-mt

The work is attributed to the sage Baudhāyana and is considered a vital source for understanding ancient Indian legal and social institutions. Despite its specialist legal/exegetical focus, it is widely consulted in studies of ancient Indian jurisprudence and dharma literature.

title: Baudhāyanaśrautasūtra

It is an authoritative manual for Vedic ritualists and a primary source for the history of ancient Indian ritual and geometry (especially the śulbasūtra passages). Its massive length (over 164,000 words) attests to its systematic coverage, but its extreme technical specialization keeps it to a specialist audience even within the Vedic subfield.

title: Baudhāyanaśrautasūtra

title: Baudhāyanaśulbasūtra

Its specialist niche within the broader Vedic corpus limits its global popularity, but it holds canonical importance within the subfield of śulba literature.

title: Baudhāyanaśulbasūtra

title: Bhāgavatapurāṇa

It is a cornerstone of medieval Vaishnavism and a canonical text in the Purāṇa corpus. title: Bhṛgusaṃhitā
author: Unknown

title: Bhṛgusaṃhitā

title: Brahmabindūpaniṣat

title: Brahmapurāṇa
author: Unknown

It encyclopedically covers cosmogony, mythology, dynastic histories, pilgrimage guides, and ritual prescriptions. As one of the eighteen major Purāṇas, it is a central text for understanding medieval Indian religious and historical traditions.

title: Brahmāṇḍapurāṇa
author: Unknown

title: Brahmāṇḍapurāṇa

It is a vast encyclopedic work covering cosmology, mythology, genealogy, and religious practices, with a particular focus on the cosmic egg (brahmāṇḍa). It is a central text for the study of Purāṇic literature and medieval Hinduism. Its length (178817 words) is substantial, reflecting its comprehensive scope.

title: Bījanighaṇṭu

title: Bṛhadāraṇyakopaniṣad
author: Unknown

Its prose dialogues and cosmological speculation explore the nature of the self (ātman) and ultimate reality (brahman), making it central to both Vedic tradition and later Hindu theology. The text is traditionally considered part of the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa and is among the earliest and most widely studied Upaniṣads.

title: Carakatattvapradīpikā

title: ChUp-Olivelle

title: Kāṭhakāraṇyaka

title: Kūrmapurāṇa
author: Unknown

With about 57,000 words, it is moderately expansive and serves as a common reference for Hindu cosmology and sectarian theology.

title: Kṛṣiparāśara

Within the broader Sanskrit corpus, it is an obscure text, of interest mainly to historians of Indian agriculture and technology. title: Kṣaṇabhaṅgasiddhi
author: Unknown

The text is specialist within the broad Sanskrit corpus, but it is a common reference in studies of Buddhist logic and ontology.

title: Lalitavistara

title: Larger Prajñāpāramitā

title: Laṅkāvatārasūtra
author: Unknown

title: Liṅgapurāṇa
author: Unknown

title: Maitrāyaṇīsaṃhitā

author: Unknown

title: Matsyapurāṇa
author: Unknown

title: Mañjuśrīmūlakalpa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Mañjuśrīmūlakalpa is an extensive Buddhist tantric text centering on the bodhisattva Mañjuśrī. It combines sūtra-style narrative, dhāraṇī (ritual incantations), maṇḍala descriptions, and instructions for rites. Composed in Sanskrit, it is a vital source for the history of Buddhist tantra, Mahāyāna ritual, and the iconography of Mañjuśrī. Its length and ritual detail make it a specialist text for scholars of Buddhist esoteric traditions.

title: Mañjuśrīmūlakalpa

title: Mokṣopāya

The work is widely studied for its literary and philosophical breadth.

title: Muṇḍakopaniṣad
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Muṇḍakopaniṣad is one of the principal or 'major' Upaniṣads, forming part of the Atharvavedic tradition and expounding the metaphysics of Brahman and Ātman through the distinctive imagery of the 'two birds' and the 'bow and arrow'. Its teaching on the hierarchy of knowledge—from lower ritual sciences to the higher knowledge of the imperishable—makes it a foundational text for Vedānta philosophy. Within the global Sanskrit corpus, the Muṇḍaka holds a common popularity tier, as it is widely cited in introductory Indian philosophy courses and Vedantic exegesis, though its readership is still narrower than the broadest popular works (epics, Bhagavadgītā).

As a foundational tantric scripture for the nondual Kashmirian Śaiva tradition, it is a common text within the Śaiva Tantra scope. title: Mālinīvijayottaratantra

title: Mānavaśrautasūtra
