source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Philosophy; dcs_subject=Yoga; dcs_time_slot=medieval; dcs_author=Bhoja; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Rājamārtaṇḍa (also known as the Bhojavṛtti) is a commentary on the Yogasūtras by Bhoja, the 11th-century Paramāra king. It is a notable medieval interpretation of Patañjali's system, offering a royal and practical perspective. While the Yogasūtras themselves are canonical, this particular commentary is of specialist interest for the history of Yoga exegesis.

title: Rājanighaṇṭu
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Lexicography; dcs_subject=Kosha; dcs_time_slot=medieval; dcs_author=Narahari; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Narahari's Rājanighaṇṭu is a medieval Sanskrit lexicon (nighaṇṭu) focused on medicinal plants, minerals, and Āyurvedic substances. It is a specialized reference work in the kośa tradition, systematizing the synonyms and properties of materia medica. The text is valuable for the history of Indian medicine and pharmacy, though it has a limited audience beyond these fields.

title: Rāmodanta
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Rāmodanta ('The End of Rāma') is a medieval Sanskrit narrative poem (ca. 2905 tokens) telling a story from the Rāma cycle. Anonymous and relatively brief, it belongs to the kāvya tradition but has a specialist readership among those studying later Rāma literature.

title: Rāmodanta
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Rāmodanta ('The End of Rāma') is a medieval Sanskrit narrative text recounting the conclusion of the Rāma story, possibly from a Purāṇic or Kāvya tradition. It is an obscure work with uncertain authorship, of interest mainly to specialists in Sanskrit narrative literature.

title: Rāṣṭrapālaparipṛcchā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Rāṣṭrapālaparipṛcchā is a Mahāyāna sūtra of moderate length (c. 12,000 words), presented as a dialogue between the Buddha and the bodhisattva Rāṣṭrapāla. It belongs to the class of Mahāyāna sūtras that emphasize faith and the prediction of future Buddhahood. Within Sanskrit Buddhist literature it is a specialist text, known among scholars of Mahāyāna sūtra literature but not widely read beyond that field.

title: Rāṣṭrapālaparipṛcchā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Rāṣṭrapālaparipṛcchā is a Mahayana Buddhist sūtra in which the Buddha answers the questions of the householder Rāṣṭrapāla. Composed in Classical Sanskrit, it belongs to the genre of Buddhist sūtra literature and is valued within Mahayana traditions for its teachings on bodhisattva conduct and emptiness.

title: SB-Eggeling
author: Unknown
classification_notes: This text, identified as 'SB-Eggeling' from a translation source, likely corresponds to a portion of the Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa, specifically the translation by Julius Eggeling. The Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa is a monumental Brāhmaṇa of the White Yajurveda, crucial for understanding Vedic ritual and mythology. This excerpt (23777 words) represents a substantial part of the text, but the exact portion is unclear from the title. It is of common interest within Vedic studies.

title: SVidhB-mt
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The work identified as SVidhB-mt is a Sanskrit text (9,479 words) likely belonging to the Śaiva tantric tradition, possibly a manual of ritual procedure (vidhi). Although its title in the source is cryptic, its classification within the tantric sphere positions it as a specialized text for practitioners and scholars of Śaiva liturgy and esoteric ritual.

title: Saddharmapuṇḍarīkasūtra
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Saddharmapuṇḍarīkasūtra (Lotus Sutra) is one of the most influential Mahayana Buddhist scriptures, central to East Asian Buddhist traditions. It is a major work in the Sanskrit Buddhist canon, widely studied in both Indological and religious studies contexts.

title: SankhGS-Oldenberg
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sankhayana Grhyasutra is a domestic ritual manual belonging to the Sankhayana school of the Rigveda. It prescribes household ceremonies and rites of passage. The text is of primary interest to specialists in Vedic ritual studies.

title: Sarvamatasaṃgraha
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sarvamatasaṃgraha is a doxographical compendium summarizing the tenets of various Indian philosophical and religious schools, likely composed in the medieval period (14th–16th century CE). Its 7,989 words present a synoptic overview of Vedānta, Jain, and other systems within a Śaiva framing. The work is specialist in the Sanskrit corpus and specialist within philosophical literature.

title: Sarvamatasaṃgraha
author: Unknown
classification_notes: Sarvamatasaṃgraha is a Sanskrit doxographical work that compiles and summarizes various philosophical and religious systems. Its authorship is uncertain, but it serves as a valuable survey of Indian philosophical traditions.

title: Sarvopakāriṇī
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sarvopakāriṇī (The All-Aiding) appears to be a late medieval Sanskrit commentary within the Vedānta tradition, possibly on a foundational text like the Brahmasūtras or a Upaniṣad, but authorship is uncertain. At about 1,700 words, it is a short gloss. Its role is specialist, being of interest primarily to scholars tracing late Vedānta commentarial literature.

title: Sarvopakāriṇī
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sarvopakāriṇī is a later Vedānta commentary, likely on a work such as the Brahmasūtras or a related text. Its authorship is not definitively established, and it is not widely cited outside advanced research into the later scholastic Vedānta traditions. It remains an obscure text known mainly to specialists in medieval Vedānta.

title: Sarvāṅgasundarā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: Aruṇadatta’s Sarvāṅgasundarā is a commentary on Vāgbhaṭa’s Aṣṭāṅgahṛdayasaṃhitā, elucidating the medical doctrines of Ayurveda. It is an important secondary source for the study of Ayurvedic literature.

title: Saṃvitsiddhi
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Buddhist Scripture; dcs_subject=Buddhist; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Yāmuna; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Saṃvitsiddhi, attributed in the DCS metadata to Yāmuna (likely a later figure, possibly influenced by the Āḻvār tradition), is a short philosophical work. The title suggests it treats the establishment (siddhi) of consciousness (saṃvit), a topic that may reflect Buddhist epistemological or idealist traditions. However, the attribution is uncertain and the text's tradition is not clearly established in the available metadata. Its brevity and ambiguous provenance make its classification tentative; it is placed here as a philosophical text of possible Buddhist affiliation.

title: Saṅghabhedavastu
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Saṅghabhedavastu (Section on Schism in the Order) is an anonymous Buddhist text from the Mūlasarvāstivāda Vinaya tradition. It is a key narrative and legal text detailing the circumstances and rules surrounding schisms within the Buddhist monastic community. Its specialized subject matter within Buddhist monastic law and narrative makes it a specialist text globally, though it is a common reference within the field of Buddhist Studies.

title: Sitātapatrā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sitātapatrā (White Umbrella) is a protective Buddhist dhāraṇī and tantric text, recited for averting calamities and harm. It belongs to the genre of Buddhist ritual protective literature. While important in certain Tibetan and Nepalese Buddhist practices, its Sanskrit version is of specialist interest to scholars of Buddhist tantric literature.

title: Sitātapatrā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sitātapatrā is a Buddhist tantric text dedicated to the goddess Sitātapatrā, known for her protective function. It belongs to the genre of dhāraṇī literature and is used in ritual contexts. While significant within Vajrayāna practice, its specialized religious and linguistic context limits its prominence in the broader Sanskrit corpus, placing it in the specialist tier.

title: Skandapurāṇa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: Skandapurāṇa is a large Shaiva Purana dedicated to the god Skanda (Karttikeya). It is a major text in Shaiva mythology and ritual.

title: Skandapurāṇa (Revākhaṇḍa)
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Revākhaṇḍa is a substantial section of the Skandapurāṇa devoted to the Narmadā river (Revā) and its sacred geography. It is an important source for Mahatmya literature and pilgrimage traditions in medieval Hinduism. Within the Purāṇa corpus it is a commonly referenced text, but it remains less canonical than the major Mahāpurāṇas.

title: Smaradīpikā
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Technical Literature; dcs_subject=Kamashastra; dcs_time_slot=medieval; dcs_author=Mīnanātha; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Smaradīpikā, attributed to Mīnanātha, is a technical treatise on erotics within the Kāmaśāstra tradition. It belongs to a genre of medieval works that elaborate on or comment upon the earlier Kāmasūtra. While a specialist text in the broader field of Sanskrit literature, it holds a moderate position among works of erotics and technical literature, offering concrete illustrations of medieval Indian social and sexual mores.

title: Spandakārikā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Spandakārikā (Verses on Vibration) by Vasugupta is a foundational text of the Pratyabhijñā school of Kashmir Śaivism. Its aphoristic verses describe the dynamic, vibratory nature of ultimate reality (Śiva). Despite its brevity, it is a major philosophical work, regularly studied by scholars of Śaiva philosophy and tantric traditions.

title: Spandakārikānirṇaya
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Spandakārikānirṇaya by Kṣemarāja is a Śaiva Tantric commentary on the Spandakārikā (c. 10th–11th century CE). Its 6,038 words elucidate the Pratyabhijñā system, analyzing the nature of vibration (spanda) as the ultimate reality. The text is specialist globally but common in Śaiva Tantra studies.

title: Sphuṭārthāvyākhyā
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Buddhist Scripture; dcs_subject=Buddhist; dcs_time_slot=medieval; dcs_author=Yaśomitra; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Sphuṭārthāvyākhyā, attributed to Yaśomitra, is a Buddhist Sanskrit commentary on the Abhidharmakośabhāṣya of Vasubandhu. It is a key exegetical work in the Sarvāstivāda-Vaibhāṣika tradition, valued by specialists in Buddhist philosophy and Abhidharma studies. Despite its importance in the scholastic tradition, it remains a niche text in the broader landscape of Sanskrit literature; the fragmentary nature and abbreviated source (only 211 words) of this edition reduce its practical usability.

title: Subhāṣitaratnakaraṇḍakathā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: Subhāṣitaratnakaraṇḍakathā is a Buddhist Sanskrit narrative collection embedding moral verses within a framing story. With a 4,754-word count, its significance is confined largely to specialists in Buddhist narrative literature and Sanskrit kathā traditions.

title: Subhāṣitaratnakaraṇḍakathā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Subhāṣitaratnakaraṇḍakathā is a Buddhist Sanskrit narrative collection, presenting moral tales and wise sayings (subhāṣita) in a framed story format. It belongs to the widespread genre of didactic story literature used for propagating Buddhist ethics. Although it serves as a valuable source for folkloric and narrative studies within Buddhism, its limited circulation and specialized doctrinal context mark it as a specialist work.

title: Sukhāvatīvyūha
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sukhāvatīvyūha, a foundational Mahāyāna sūtra centered on the Pure Land of Amitābha, is a key text in Buddhist devotional and soteriological traditions. Originating in classical India, this sūtra’s visionary portrayal of buddha-field practice has made it a canonical reference in East Asian Buddhism. Its approximately 11,000-word length reflects a moderately extended scriptural exposition.

title: Sukhāvatīvyūha
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sukhāvatīvyūha (the Larger Pure Land Sūtra) is one of the most influential Mahāyāna Buddhist sūtras, describing the pure land of Amitābha. It is foundational to East Asian Pure Land Buddhism and widely studied in Buddhist studies globally. Its popularity in the Sanskrit tradition is common-tier, but within Buddhist scripture it ranks as a major text.

title: Svacchandatantra
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Svacchandatantra is a major Śaiva Tantra text, revered in the Kashmir Śaiva tradition. Its 52,454 words constitute a comprehensive scriptural and ritual compendium. Though specialist in the broader Sanskrit corpus, within Tantric and Śaiva studies it is a canonical work of theology, cosmology, and practice.

title: Svacchandatantra
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Svacchandatantra is a major scripture of the Śaiva Siddhānta tradition, presenting the theology, ritual, and yoga centered on the deity Svacchanda Bhairava. It is an extensive tantra that has been foundational for later Śaiva practice and philosophy, with substantial commentarial traditions. It is widely cited in the study of Tantric Śaivism.

title: Svayaṃbhupurāṇa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Svayaṃbhupurāṇa is a Jain Puranic text that narrates the lives of the Jain Tīrthaṅkaras, with a focus on Ādinātha (Svayaṃbhu). It belongs to the genre of Jain Purāṇas, which blend religious instruction with mythological narratives, and is important for the study of Jain devotional literature.

title: Svayaṃbhupurāṇa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: Classification: The 'Svayaṃbhupurāṇa' is a Jain puranic text of moderate length and significant doctrinal value within Jain literature, serving as a quasi-scriptural narrative work. While relatively obscure on a global Sanskrit scale, it holds a common position within the Jain purana sub-tradition. The author is unknown (traditional sacred literature). The work dates to the medieval period.

title: SvetUp-Olivelle
author: Unknown
classification_notes: Śvetāśvatara Upaniṣad is a major Upaniṣad of the Krishna Yajurveda, focusing on the philosophy of the Supreme Being Śiva. It is a key text in Vedanta and Hindu theology.

title: Sāmavidhānabrāhmaṇa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sāmavidhānabrāhmaṇa is a late Vedic Brahmana text associated with the Sāmaveda, dealing with the application of Sāman chants for specific desires and rites. It is a specialized text within the Vedic literature, consulted primarily for studies of Vedic ritual and magic.

title: Sāmavidhānabrāhmaṇa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sāmavidhānabrāhmaṇa (ca. 5212 tokens) is a Brāhmaṇa text of the Sāmaveda, dealing with the ritual application of sāman chants. It is an important source for Vedic liturgy and studied by specialists in Sāmavedic tradition.

title: Sāmavidhānabrāhmaṇa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sāmavidhānabrāhmaṇa is a Vedic prose text belonging to the Sāmaveda tradition, providing ritual prescriptions (vidhāna) for the use of Sāman chants in achieving specific worldly and otherworldly aims. With 4,718 tokens it is a moderate-length Brāhmaṇa. As a specialist text within the entire Sanskrit corpus, it is a common reference among scholars of Vedic ritual literature and Sāmavedic exegesis.

title: Sārdhatriśatikālottarāgama
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sārdhatriśatikālottarāgama is a Śaiva Āgama text, presenting ritual, cosmology, and spiritual practice within the Kālottara tradition. Though less famous than major tantras, it is an integral part of the Śaiva scriptural corpus and is studied by specialists in Tantric Śaivism and ritual literature.

title: Sārdhatriśatikālottarāgama
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sārdhatriśatikālottarāgama is a Śaiva Āgama text of moderate length, presenting ritual procedures and liturgical instructions within the Brahmanical tantric tradition. It is an important source for the study of Śaiva ritual and temple practice, though its readership is largely confined to specialists in tantric literature and Indian ritual studies. Within the broader Sanskrit corpus it is a specialist work.

title: Sātvatatantra
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sātvatatantra is an early Pāñcarātra Āgama text revered primarily within the Śrīvaiṣṇava tradition and sometimes classified under the tantric corpus. It presents foundational theology, ritual practice, and cosmology centered on Viṣṇu as the supreme being, with a prominence in Śaiva-tinged repertoire. Though significant as a key source for early Pāñcarātra scholarship, its relative obscurity outside the specialist domain of Indian tantric and Āgamic studies positions it as a specialist text within the global Sanskrit corpus.

title: Sāṃkhyakārikābhāṣya
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Philosophy; dcs_subject=Samkhya; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Gauḍapāda; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Attributed to Gauḍapāda, the Sāṃkhyakārikābhāṣya is an early commentary on Īśvarakṛṣṇa’s Sāṃkhyakārikā. Its 7,085-word exposition provides a rare early witness to Sāṃkhya exegesis, making it a specialist resource within the history of Indian philosophy.

title: Sāṃkhyaparibhāṣā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: Sāṃkhyaparibhāṣā is a philosophical treatise on Sāṃkhya doctrine. It is of moderate importance within Sāṃkhya studies but less known outside that tradition.

title: Sāṃkhyaparibhāṣā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sāṃkhyaparibhāṣā is a late exposition of Sāṃkhya philosophy, summarizing its metaphysics of prakṛti and puruṣa in clear, pedagogical language. It is a useful but not widely known introductory manual, studied by specialists in later Sāṃkhya and the history of philosophical systems.

title: Sāṃkhyasūtravivaraṇa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sāṃkhyasūtravivaraṇa is a 1,776-word commentary on the Sāṃkhyasūtras, likely composed in the medieval period. It presents the dualist Sāṃkhya system with Vedāntic interpretive moves. The text is specialist in the Sanskrit corpus and specialist within Sāṃkhya and Vedānta studies.

title: Sāṃkhyasūtravivaraṇa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Sāṃkhyasūtravivaraṇa is a commentary on the Sāṃkhyasūtras, a foundational text of the Sāṃkhya system. The authorship of this vivaraṇa is uncertain, and it is a relatively obscure work within the Sāṃkhya exegetical tradition, consulted mainly by specialists investigating the later scholastic development of the darśana.

title: Sāṃkhyatattvakaumudī
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Philosophy; dcs_subject=Samkhya; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Vācaspatimiśra; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Vācaspatimiśra's Sāṃkhyatattvakaumudī is a celebrated commentary on Īśvarakṛṣṇa's Sāṃkhyakārikā. It is a key text for understanding classical Sāṃkhya philosophy, widely used in scholarly study. Within the field of Sāṃkhya it is a major reference, though Sāṃkhya's narrower audience place it in the common tier for philosophy overall.

title: Sūryasiddhānta
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Astronomy and Mathematics; dcs_subject=Jyotisha; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Sūryasiddhānta is one of the most influential and widely cited works of Indian astronomy (jyotiṣa). Though its traditional origins are ancient, the extant version dates from the classical period. It presents a geocentric model of the solar system, with mathematical methods for calculating planetary positions, eclipses, and the calendar. Despite its moderate word count in the DCS corpus, the Sūryasiddhānta has been a standard text in the Indian astronomical tradition and has attracted modern scholarly attention.

title: Sūryaśataka
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Kavya; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Mayūra; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Sūryaśataka is a classical Sanskrit stotra by Mayūra, consisting of a hundred verses in praise of the Sun god (Sūrya). It is a fine example of ornate devotional poetry and is well known within the stotra tradition, but is a specialist text in the broader Sanskrit corpus.

title: Sūryaśatakaṭīkā
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Kavya; dcs_time_slot=medieval; dcs_author=Tribhuvanapāla; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Sūryaśatakaṭīkā is a commentary by Tribhuvanapāla on the Sūryaśataka, a Sanskrit poem in praise of the sun god. The commentary elucidates the poetic and devotional elements of the base text and reflects the medieval scholastic tradition of kāvya commentary.

title: TS-mt
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Taittirīyasaṃhitā (here TS-mt) is the main saṃhitā (mantra collection) of the Taittirīya (Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda) school, containing both mantras for ritual use and the earliest layers of Brāhmaṇa-like exposition integrated within the mantra sections. It is foundational for Vedic ritual studies and the history of early Indian prose. While it holds common popularity within the Vedic scope, its global reach across the Sanskrit corpus is specialist, as its technical ritual content and archaic language restrict engagement to specialists of Vedic philology, Indo-Iranian linguistics, and ritual theory.

title: Taittirīyabrāhmaṇa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Taittirīyabrāhmaṇa is a prose text of the Krishna Yajurveda, providing explanations of the sacrificial rituals and mythological narratives. It is a foundational work for the study of Vedic ritual and exegesis, widely cited in later tradition.

title: Taittirīyasaṃhitā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Taittirīyasaṃhitā is a recension of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda, containing mantras and ritual instructions. It is a primary source for the study of Vedic religion and ritual.

title: Taittirīyasaṃhitā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Taittirīyasaṃhitā is a key recension of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda, one of the four Vedas. It is a collection of mantras and ritual instructions used in Vedic sacrifices, and it is foundational for the study of Vedic religion, language, and culture. As a primary Vedic text, it is widely studied in Indology and religious studies, though its technical content places it in the common rather than major tier for general readers.

title: Taittirīyasaṃhitā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Taittirīyasaṃhitā is a primary recension of the Black Yajurveda, containing mantras and prose explanations for Vedic sacrifices. It is a major text in the Sanskrit corpus and canonical within Vedic literature, central to the study of ancient Indian ritual and language.

title: Taittirīyopaniṣad
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Taittirīyopaniṣad is a major Upaniṣad of the Yajurveda, belonging to the Taittirīya school. It is divided into three sections (valli) covering phonetics, ethics, and metaphysics, including the famous declaration 'Tat tvam asi' (That thou art) in its philosophical portion. It is a central text for the study of Vedānta and early Upaniṣadic thought, widely read and commented upon. Its length (4037 words) is typical for a principal Upaniṣad.

title: Taittirīyāraṇyaka
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Taittirīyāraṇyaka is a major Vedic Āraṇyaka text belonging to the Taittirīya school of the Kṛṣṇa Yajurveda. With 12992 words, it comprises ritual instructions, cosmological speculations, and esoteric interpretations of sacrifice. As an anonymous traditional work dated to the early first millennium BCE, it is essential for scholars of Vedic ritual and theology, though it holds a specialist readership.

title: Tantrasāra
author: Unknown
classification_notes: Abhinavagupta's Tantrasāra (Essence of the Tantras) is a foundational summary of Kashmir Śaiva Tantra philosophy and practice, composed in the 10th–11th century. It distills the teachings of the more voluminous Tantrāloka into a manageable compendium, making it a widely studied text in Tantric studies and Indian philosophy. It is a key work for understanding the Pratyabhijñā and Trika systems.

title: Tantrākhyāyikā
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Narrative Literature; dcs_subject=Katha; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Tantrākhyāyikā is a recension of the Pañcatantra, a collection of fables and wisdom tales. It is a key text in the narrative literature (kathā) tradition and has been influential in the transmission of Indian fables to the West.

title: Tarkabhāṣā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Tarkabhāṣā ('Language of Logic') is a manual of Navya-Nyāya logic, presenting the technical terminology and argument forms of this school in a systematic way. It is a moderately sized work (c. 12,400 words) and serves as an introductory or intermediate text for students of Nyāya. Its readership is almost exclusively specialists in Indian logic and philosophy.

title: Tarkabhāṣā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: Tarkabhāṣā, often attributed to Keśava Miśra, is a concise Nyāya manual. It serves as an introductory text for students of Indian logic and is moderately well-known in scholarly circles.

title: Tattvamīmāṃsā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Tattvamīmāṃsā (Investigation of Reality) is a philosophical treatise of unknown authorship, likely from the medieval period. It appears to engage with Advaita Vedānta or related traditions, analyzing fundamental principles (tattvas). This short text of about 2,930 words is known only to specialists in later Indian philosophy, and its precise doctrinal affiliations require further study.

title: Tattvamīmāṃsā
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Tattvamīmāṃsā is a minor philosophical treatise belonging to the Mīmāṃsā tradition. Its author is uncertain. This text is of specific interest to historians of Indian philosophy, especially those working on the intersection of Mīmāṃsā and Vedānta. Its obscurity and technical nature limit its readership to a specialist circle.

title: Tattvasamāsasūtravṛtti
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Tattvasamāsasūtravṛtti is a commentary on the Tattvasamāsa Sūtra, a foundational text of the Sāṃkhya philosophical system. The work is anonymous and likely medieval, serving as an exegetical aid for students of Sāṃkhya.

title: Tattvasamāsasūtravṛtti
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Tattvasamāsasūtravṛtti is a commentary (vṛtti) on the Tattvasamāsa sūtras, a concise Sāṃkhya text. The work’s authorship remains uncertain, though it likely belongs to the later medieval scholastic tradition of Sāṃkhya exegesis. Its relatively short length (about 2,600 words) and technical focus orient it toward specialist study.

title: Tattvavaiśāradī
author: Unknown
classification_notes: Tattvavaiśāradī, traditionally attributed to Vācaspati Miśra, is a commentary on the Yogasūtra. Despite its philosophical importance, its short length and specialist readership limit its broader popularity.

title: Toḍalatantra
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Toḍalatantra is a Śākta Tantric text of modest length (3,896 words) that belongs to the later Tantric corpus. It is of interest primarily to specialists in Tantric literature and Śākta ritual traditions.

title: Trikāṇḍaśeṣa

title: Āryānityatāsūtra

title: Āśvalāyanaśrautasūtra
author: Unknown

It systematically covers the procedures for śrauta (public) sacrifices, including the agniṣṭoma, ukthya, and other soma offerings. Along with the Śāṅkhāyanaśrautasūtra, it is one of the two principal śrautasūtras of the Rigveda and is an essential source for the study of Vedic ritual performance and the development of the kalpa tradition.

It is a critical source for understanding ancient and early medieval Vedic ritual practice.

title: Āṅgirasasmṛti

Though its authority is less than that of the classical Dharmaśāstra of Manu or Yājñavalkya, it is frequently consulted within the specialist field of Dharmashastra for comparative studies on penance and socio-religious norms in early medieval India. It is a common text within the Dharmashastra scope but remains specialist across the full Sanskrit corpus.

title: Ṛgveda
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Ṛgveda is the oldest and most foundational text of the Vedic corpus and of the entire Sanskrit literary tradition. It is a collection of 1,028 hymns arranged in ten maṇḍalas, composed over centuries in early Vedic Sanskrit. With c. 322,800 words, it is a massive and foundational work. Its influence on Indian religion, philosophy, literature, and culture is unparalleled, and it is studied globally across multiple disciplines. It is justifiably placed in the canonical tier, both globally and within its discovery group.

author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Ṛgvedakhilāni are a collection of supplementary hymns (khila) to the Ṛgveda, traditionally considered as appendices to the main Saṃhitā. They contain hymns of various content, including some that are later or less canonical. This text is of specialist interest within Vedic studies, particularly for the study of the Ṛgvedic corpus and its transmission. Its length (13847 words) is substantial for a collection of khila hymns.

title: Ṛgvedavedāṅgajyotiṣa
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Astronomy and Mathematics; dcs_subject=Jyotisha; dcs_time_slot=early; dcs_author=Lagadha; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: One of the earliest Indian astronomical texts, the Ṛgvedavedāṅgajyotiṣa is a short manual of calendrical calculation (jyotiṣa). Attributed to Lagadha, it is a vedāṅga (limb of the Veda) that prescribes rules for determining the auspicious times for Vedic rituals. Despite its brevity, it is a foundational text for the history of Indian astronomy and is well-known among specialists.

title: Ṛgvidhāna
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Vedic Ritual and Exegesis; dcs_subject=Kalpa; dcs_time_slot=early; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Ṛgvidhāna is a late Vedic or early post-Vedic kalpa text that prescribes the ritual and magical applications of Ṛgvedic mantras. Though compact (ca. 1400 words), it bridges the worlds of śrauta ritual and popular domestic practice. Its significance is felt mainly within the specialist domain of Vedic ritual literature and the study of mantra-śāstra.

title: Ṛtusaṃhāra
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Kavya; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Kālidāsa; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Ṛtusaṃhāra, traditionally attributed to Kālidāsa, is a lyrical poem describing the six seasons. Though authorship is debated, it remains a widely read and cherished work of Sanskrit poetry.

title: ṢB-mt
author: Unknown
classification_notes: This text, identified as 'ṢB-mt' from a translation source, likely corresponds to a portion of the Ṣaḍviṃśa Brāhmaṇa or a related Sāmavedic Brāhmaṇa. The exact identification is uncertain, but the text is clearly a Brāhmaṇa of the Sāmaveda. This excerpt (12585 words) is of specialist interest to scholars of Vedic ritual and Sāmavedic literature.

title: Ṣaḍviṃśabrāhmaṇa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Ṣaḍviṃśabrāhmaṇa, literally the 'Brāhmaṇa of Twenty-Six,' is a late Vedic text associated with the Sāmaveda. As a Brahmana concerned with Vedic ritual procedure, it is a primary source for specialists in Vedic studies and the history of Indian ritual but has limited readership outside this field.

title: Ṣaḍviṃśabrāhmaṇa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Ṣaḍviṃśabrāhmaṇa is an appendix to the Pañcaviṃśabrāhmaṇa, containing additional ritual specifications. It is less widely studied but valuable for detailed Vedic ritual analysis.

title: Ṣaḍviṃśabrāhmaṇa
author: Unknown
classification_notes: The Ṣaḍviṃśabrāhmaṇa is a late Vedic prose text associated with the Sāmaveda, containing ritual explanations and legends. It is of specialist interest for Vedic studies and Indology, with limited circulation beyond that domain.

title: Ṭikanikayātrā
author: Unknown
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Astronomy and Mathematics; dcs_subject=Jyotisha; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Varāhamihira; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Classification: The 'Ṭikanikayātrā' is a minor astrological/astronomical manual attributed to Varāhamihira, dealing with predictive itineraries. Its brevity (1124 words) and specialized nature mark it as an obscure work; it holds relevance only to specialists in classical Indian astronomy, remaining virtually unknown outside of that subfield.

title: Śivastotrāvali
author: Utpaladeva
classification_notes: The Śivastotrāvali by Utpaladeva is a collection of devotional hymns to Śiva, composed in the Pratyabhijñā tradition of Kashmir Śaivism. It is a key stotra text for the study of Śaiva bhakti and Tantric philosophy, with a significant but specialist readership.

title: Śivastotrāvali
author: Utpaladeva
classification_notes: The Śivastotrāvali ('Garland of Hymns to Śiva') by Utpaladeva is a celebrated collection of devotional hymns (stotras) in praise of Śiva. Utpaladeva was a central figure in the Pratyabhijñā school of Kashmir Śaivism, and his hymns blend profound philosophical insight with intense personal devotion. This work is a key text for the study of Śaiva bhakti and the theological literature of Kashmir Śaivism.

title: Nyāyavārttikatātparyaṭīkā
author: Vacaspatimiśra
classification_notes: Vacaspatimiśra's Nyāyavārttikatātparyaṭīkā is a critical sub-commentary on Uddyotakara's Nyāyavārttika, interpreting the Nyāya Sūtras. It is a sophisticated work of Nyāya philosophy, known for its clarity and depth, and remains a standard reference in the tradition.

title: Nyāyavārttikatātparyaṭīkā
author: Vacaspatimiśra
classification_notes: The Nyāyavārttikatātparyaṭīkā is a substantial commentary by the great Nyāya philosopher Vācaspatimiśra, written on Uddyotakara's Nyāyavārttika, which itself comments on Gautama's Nyāyasūtras. At over 100,000 words, it is a magisterial work of philosophical exegesis, clarifying and extending the arguments of the Nyāya school. It is a central text for the study of classical Indian logic and epistemology.

title: Subhāṣitāvali
author: Vallabhadeva
classification_notes: The Subhāṣitāvali, compiled by Vallabhadeva, is a notable anthology of subhāṣitas (wise or elegant sayings) drawn from a wide range of Sanskrit poets and works. It serves as a treasury of classical and early medieval Sanskrit verse, preserving both celebrated and rare excerpts. Valuable for literary history and the study of gnomic poetry, it is a standard reference among scholars of Sanskrit kāvya, yet remains a specialist work outside the immediate circle of researchers.

title: Subhāṣitāvali
author: Vallabhadeva
classification_notes: Vallabhadeva's Subhāṣitāvali is a celebrated anthology of Sanskrit subhāṣitas (wise sayings) and courtly poetry, organized by theme. It draws from a wide range of authors and is a valuable source for Sanskrit kāvya and moral instruction.

title: Laghusiddhāntakaumudī
author: Varadarāja
classification_notes: The Laghusiddhāntakaumudī by Varadarāja is a 25,091-word abridgment of Bhaṭṭoji Dīkṣita's Siddhāntakaumudī, a classic commentary on Pāṇini's Aṣṭādhyāyī. This abridgment is designed as a pedagogical text for learners of Sanskrit grammar, presenting the key rules in a streamlined and accessible sequence. It is widely used in traditional and modern Sanskrit instruction and is a major reference in the Pāṇinian grammatical tradition.

title: Laghusiddhāntakaumudī
author: Varadarāja
classification_notes: Classification: The 'Laghusiddhāntakaumudī' of Varadarāja is an abbreviated version of Bhaṭṭoji Dīkṣita's Siddhāntakaumudī, serving as a pedagogical grammar of Sanskrit. It is specialist globally but major within the Sanskrit grammar tradition, being a standard text for intermediate learners.

title: Śāṅkhāyanaśrautasūtrabhāṣya
author: Varadattasuta
classification_notes: Varadattasuta’s Śāṅkhāyanaśrautasūtrabhāṣya is a commentary (bhāṣya) on the Śāṅkhāyana Śrautasūtra, a key text of the R̥gvedic ritual corpus. At about 40,000 words, it provides extensive explanation of Vedic sacrificial procedure and is crucial for specialists in Vedic ritual exegesis.

title: Śāṅkhāyanaśrautasūtrabhāṣya
author: Varadattasuta
classification_notes: Varadattasuta’s Śāṅkhāyanaśrautasūtrabhāṣya is a detailed 34,365-word commentary on the Śāṅkhāyana Śrautasūtra. Its specialist audience lies among historians of Vedic ritual and those engaged in the exegesis of the Śrauta tradition.

title: Śatagāthā
author: Vararuci
classification_notes: Vararuci's Śatagāthā is a collection of one hundred stanzas, traditionally attributed to the grammarian Vararuci. It is a minor poetic work, of interest mainly for its stylistic and linguistic features.

title: Śatagāthā
author: Vararuci
classification_notes: The Śatagāthā (Hundred Verses), attributed to Vararuci, is a collection of 1,727 words of poetic aphorisms and observations on worldly wisdom, often in the nīti genre. It belongs to the subhāṣita (wise saying) tradition and is of interest to specialists in Sanskrit didactic verse and gnomic poetry.

title: Bṛhatsaṃhitā
author: Varāhamihira
classification_notes: The Bṛhatsaṃhitā (Great Compendium) is an encyclopedic work by the celebrated 6th-century astronomer-astrologer Varāhamihira. With over 50,000 words, it covers an immense range of topics including astronomy, astrology, omens, meteorology, architecture, gemology, and agriculture. It is one of the most important and widely cited Sanskrit works on the astral sciences, offering invaluable insight into classical Indian scientific and cultural knowledge.

title: Bṛhatsaṃhitā
author: Varāhamihira
classification_notes: Bṛhatsaṃhitā is a monumental Sanskrit compendium on astronomy, astrology, divination, omens, and natural sciences by Varāhamihira (6th century CE). It is a canonical text in Indian astronomy and a major source for early medieval Indian science.

title: Yogayātrā
author: Varāhamihira
classification_notes: Varāhamihira's Yogayātrā (Expedition of Conjunctions) is a seminal work on Indian astrological election and military astrology (jayayātrā), composed around the 6th century CE. It systematizes celestial omens and planetary conjunctions for timing campaigns and other undertakings. It is a well-known text in the history of Indian astronomy and astrology, frequently cited by specialists in the field.

title: Yogayātrā
author: Varāhamihira
classification_notes: Yogayātrā is one of Varāhamihira's major works on horā (astrology), focusing on electional astrology (yogayātrā) for journeying. It forms part of his Pañcasiddhāntikā corpus and is a key text in the jyotiṣa tradition for understanding the astrological principles of travel.

title: Abhidharmakośa
author: Vasubandhu
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Buddhist Scripture; dcs_subject=Buddhist; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Vasubandhu; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Abhidharmakośa by Vasubandhu (c. 4th–5th century CE) is a foundational Sanskrit text of Buddhist Abhidharma. Its 639 words as represented here may be an excerpt or fragment, but the full work is a systematic treatise on phenomena, karma, and the path. The work is globally common and major within Buddhist philosophy.

title: Abhidharmakośabhāṣya
author: Vasubandhu
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Buddhist Scripture; dcs_subject=Buddhist; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Vasubandhu; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Abhidharmakośabhāṣya by Vasubandhu is a seminal text of Buddhist Abhidharma philosophy, presenting a systematic exposition of doctrine. Despite the DCS entry showing only 75 words (likely an extract), the work as a whole is a common, widely studied text across the Sanskrit philosophical corpus and a major work within Buddhist Abhidharma.

title: Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkārabhāṣya
author: Vasubandhu
classification_notes: Vasubandhu's Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkārabhāṣya is a commentary (bhāṣya) on the Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra, a key Yogācāra text. Attributed to the great Buddhist philosopher Vasubandhu, this work (31,596 words) is a central text for the study of Yogācāra philosophy. It is a common text within the specialized field of Buddhist Philosophy, but its global popularity is specialist, as it is not part of the core curriculum for general Sanskrit literature.

title: Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkārabhāṣya
author: Vasubandhu
classification_notes: The Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkārabhāṣya is a commentary (bhāṣya) by Vasubandhu on the Mahāyānasūtrālaṃkāra, a key Yogācāra work attributed to Asaṅga. This substantial text (c. 35,500 tokens) elucidates the Mahāyāna path and buddha-bodhisattva theory, making it essential for the study of Yogācāra doctrinal exegesis. As part of Vasubandhu's commentarial corpus it commands a common level of recognition both within Buddhist philosophy and in the broader Sanskrit philosophical landscape.

title: Triṃśikāvijñaptikārikā
author: Vasubandhu
classification_notes: Vasubandhu's Triṃśikāvijñaptikārikā is a foundational Yogācāra text summarizing the doctrine of consciousness-only in thirty verses. Its concise philosophical argument and importance in Buddhist scholasticism make it widely studied within Buddhist philosophy and comparative philosophy. It commands a common readership across the Sanskrit corpus, being a standard in graduate curricula but not at the canonical level of major epics.

title: Triṃśikāvijñaptikārikā
author: Vasubandhu
classification_notes: Vasubandhu's Triṃśikāvijñaptikārikā (Thirty Verses on Consciousness-Only) is a foundational text of the Yogācāra school of Mahāyāna Buddhism. It presents the doctrine of vijñapti-mātra (representation-only) in thirty concise verses. This work is of major importance within Buddhist philosophy and is widely studied by scholars of Yogācāra. Its short length (392 words) is characteristic of a kārikā text.

title: Haṃsasaṃdeśa
author: Vedānta Deśika
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Kavya; dcs_subject=Kavya; dcs_time_slot=medieval; dcs_author=Vedānta Deśika; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Vedānta Deśika's Haṃsasaṃdeśa is an elegant medieval Sanskrit kāvya modelled on Kālidāsa's Meghadūta, in which a messenger swan carries a love message. Though fine poetry, its restricted scope and derivative genre make it a specialist work within the broader Sanskrit literary tradition.

title: Tarkasopāna
author: Vidyākaraśānti
classification_notes: A philosophical manual of Buddhist logic and epistemology (pramāṇa) written by Vidyākaraśānti. The Tarkasopāna ('Ladder of Reasoning') is a scholastic text that systematically presents arguments of the Buddhist Nyāya school. It is a specialist work of interest primarily to scholars of Buddhist philosophy and Indian logic.

title: Tarkasopāna
author: Vidyākaraśānti
classification_notes: Tarkasopāna is a Buddhist logical treatise by Vidyākaraśānti, presenting a step-by-step introduction to reasoning. It is valued in the Buddhist philosophical tradition, especially in Tibetan scholasticism.

title: Sāṃkhyasāra
author: Vijñānabhikṣu
classification_notes: A concise summary of Sāṃkhya philosophy written by Vijñānabhikṣu, a renowned thinker who attempted to synthesize Sāṃkhya, Yoga, and Vedānta. The Sāṃkhyasāra ('Essence of Sāṃkhya') is a clear and systematic exposition of the school's core doctrines. It is a specialist text, but an important one for understanding the later developments of the Sāṃkhya tradition.

title: Sāṃkhyasāra
author: Vijñānabhikṣu
classification_notes: Vijñānabhikṣu's Sāṃkhyasāra is a concise exposition of Sāṃkhya philosophy, written from a synthetic perspective that also engages with Yoga and Vedānta. It is a relatively late work that aims to distill the essential doctrines of the dualistic Sāṃkhya school. It is read in the context of the consolidation of classical darśanas in early modernity.

title: Mitākṣarā
author: Vijñāneśvara
classification_notes: The Mitākṣarā is a celebrated legal commentary by Vijñāneśvara on the Yājñavalkyasmṛti. It became authoritative across much of India for Hindu inheritance law. It is a common work within the broader Sanskrit tradition and major within the Dharmashastra commentarial genre.

title: Mitākṣarā
author: Vijñāneśvara
classification_notes: The Mitākṣarā, authored by Vijñāneśvara, is one of the most celebrated and authoritative commentaries on the Yājñavalkya Smṛti, a foundational text of Dharmashastra. It formed the basis for the legal system in much of medieval and early modern India, especially in the Bombay Presidency. This work is a major reference point for scholars of Hindu law and Indian social history, achieving a common popularity tier within its narrow scope.

title: Abhidharmadīpa
author: Vimalamitra
classification_notes: Abhidharmadīpa by Vimalamitra is an important Buddhist Abhidharma text from the classical period. It is a key work within the Sarvāstivāda tradition, though less widely read outside specialist Buddhist studies.

title: Abhidharmadīpa
author: Vimalamitra
classification_notes: The Abhidharmadīpa attributed to Vimalamitra is a Buddhist philosophical treatise in the Abhidharma tradition, likely of the Sarvāstivāda school. Its 6,762 words suggest a concise critical text. Valued in Buddhist philosophical studies for its early medieval doctrinal exposition.

title: Sāhityadarpaṇa
author: Viśvanāthakavirāja
classification_notes: The Sāhityadarpaṇa ('Mirror of Literature') by Viśvanāthakavirāja is one of the most influential and comprehensive treatises on Sanskrit poetics (alaṃkāraśāstra). It systematically covers dramaturgy, rasa theory, figures of speech, and poetic forms. It is a fundamental textbook in the study of Sanskrit literature and aesthetics, read widely by students and scholars alike.

title: Sāhityadarpaṇa
author: Viśvanāthakavirāja
classification_notes: The Sāhityadarpaṇa ('Mirror of Composition') by Viśvanāthakavirāja (ca. 10,939 tokens) is a comprehensive medieval Sanskrit treatise on poetics and literary theory. It is a major work in Sanskrit aesthetic theory, widely used in the study of kāvya and alamkāraśāstra.

title: Camatkāracandrikā
author: Viśveśvarakavicandra
classification_notes: Viśveśvarakavicandra's Camatkāracandrikā is a treatise on alaṃkāraśāstra, the science of poetic figures. It analyzes rhetorical ornaments and is a resource for scholars of Sanskrit literary theory.

title: Camatkāracandrikā
author: Viśveśvarakavicandra
classification_notes: Viśveśvarakavicandra's Camatkāracandrikā is a work in the Sanskrit alaṃkāraśāstra tradition, analyzing figures of speech and poetic ornamentation. It explores the concept of camatkāra (aesthetic wonder) as a central criterion of literary beauty. The work is valued by specialists in Sanskrit poetics and literary theory.

title: Bhagavadgītā
author: Vyāsa
classification_notes: The Bhagavadgītā (BhG) is a 700-verse poem within the Mahābhārata (6.23–40). Attributed to Vyāsa, it is a central Vedāntic, yogic, and dharmic dialogue between Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna. As a standalone text of 13,361 words, it is the most widely read and commented-upon philosophical poem in the Sanskrit corpus, canonical globally and within the epic tradition.

title: Mahābhārata
author: Vyāsa
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Epic Literature; dcs_subject=Mahabharata; dcs_time_slot=epic; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Mahābhārata is one of the two great Sanskrit epics and a foundational text of Indian civilization. Attributed to the sage Vyāsa, it is a monumental work of nearly 1.8 million words, encompassing narrative, philosophy, ethics, and theology. Its influence on Indian literature, religion, and culture is unparalleled, and it is read and studied across the world.

title: Bhāmati
author: Vācaspatimiśra
classification_notes: The Bhāmatī by Vācaspatimiśra is a major early medieval commentary on Śaṅkara's Brahmasūtrabhāṣya, central to the Advaita Vedanta tradition. It is a widely studied work across the Sanskrit philosophical corpus and canonical within Vedanta.

title: Bhāmati
author: Vācaspatimiśra
classification_notes: Vācaspatimiśra’s Bhāmati is a celebrated commentary on Śaṅkara’s Brahmasūtrabhāṣya, constituting a major landmark in Advaita Vedānta exegesis. Its length (about 90,000 words) supports a thorough engagement and makes it a core text for advanced study of Vedānta philosophy.

title: Mānamanohara
author: Vādivāgīśvara
classification_notes: The Mānamanohara by Vādivāgīśvara is a work on poetics or literary appreciation, possibly a commentary or independent treatise on figures of speech and aesthetic judgment. Its obscurity outside specialist circles is reflected in its minimal attestation.

title: Mānamanohara
author: Vādivāgīśvara
classification_notes: A Jain philosophical work on the theory of knowledge (pramāṇa) attributed to Vādivāgīśvara. The Mānamanohara ('Delight of the Means of Knowledge') is a technical treatise in the Jain tradition of logic and epistemology. It is of specialist interest within Jain philosophy and Indian logic.

title: Aṣṭāṅgahṛdayasaṃhitā
author: Vāgbhaṭa
classification_notes: Vāgbhaṭa’s Aṣṭāṅgahṛdayasaṃhitā is a seminal work of Ayurveda, summarizing the eight branches of medical knowledge in verse. It ranks alongside the Caraka and Suśruta saṃhitās as a foundational text of the tradition.

title: Aṣṭāṅgasaṃgraha
author: Vāgbhaṭa
classification_notes: The Aṣṭāṅgasaṃgraha by Vāgbhaṭa (the elder) is a classical Ayurvedic compendium that systematically presents the eight branches (aṣṭāṅga) of Indian medicine in 1157 words of condensed but influential text. This excerpt or distillation of the larger work is valued by historians of medicine as a clear summary of classical Ayurvedic theory and practice.

title: Mṛtyuvañcanopadeśa
author: Vāgīśvarakīrti
classification_notes: Vāgīśvarakīrti's Mṛtyuvañcanopadeśa is a Buddhist tantric manual focusing on rites to overcome death. It belongs to the Vajrayāna tradition and provides instructions on meditation, mantra, and ritual, reflecting the esoteric practices of late Indian Buddhism.

title: Mṛtyuvañcanopadeśa
author: Vāgīśvarakīrti
classification_notes: The Mṛtyuvañcanopadeśa by Vāgīśvarakīrti is a Buddhist Tantric work teaching the conquest of death, a classic specialist text within the Indian Buddhist tantric tradition. It has moderate significance for researchers of Buddhist esoteric literature.

title: Rāmāyaṇa
author: Vālmīki
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Epic Literature; dcs_subject=Ramayana; dcs_time_slot=epic; dcs_author=Valmīki; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Vālmīki's Rāmāyaṇa is a foundational epic of Indian civilization, telling the story of Rāma's exile, the abduction of Sītā, and his quest to rescue her. It is the earliest known Sanskrit kāvya (ādikāvya) and has exerted immense influence on literature, religion, art, and morality across South and Southeast Asia. It is one of the most central texts in the entire Sanskrit corpus.

title: Dvayasaṃpattivārttika
author: Vāmanadatta
classification_notes: Vāmanadatta's Dvayasaṃpattivārttika is a brief philosophical commentary (vārttika) on the concept of the 'attainment of duality' within the Kashmir Śaiva tradition. It is a highly specialized text, of interest primarily to scholars of Śaiva Tantric philosophy and the works of Vāmanadatta. Its very short length (332 words) suggests a concise, possibly fragmentary, treatment of a specific doctrinal point.

title: Dvayasaṃpattivārttika
author: Vāmanadatta
classification_notes: The Dvayasaṃpattivārttika by Vāmanadatta is a brief commentarial work (vārttika) on the Dvayasaṃpatti, a Śaiva tantric text. It belongs to the Śaiva Siddhānta tradition and addresses ritual and philosophical aspects of attaining dual accomplishment (dvaya-sampatti). Its brevity and specialized focus make it a text of interest primarily for scholars of Śaiva tantric literature and its commentarial traditions.

title: Svabodhodayamañjarī
author: Vāmanadatta
classification_notes: The Svabodhodayamañjarī by Vāmanadatta is a short philosophical poem expounding the non-dualistic Śaiva doctrine of self-knowledge. It is a specialist work within the Pratyabhijñā tradition of Kashmiri Śaivism.

title: Svabodhodayamañjarī
author: Vāmanadatta
classification_notes: Vāmanadatta's Svabodhodayamañjarī is a short philosophical poem expounding Advaita Vedānta through the metaphor of a blossoming creeper of self-knowledge. Though it draws on poetic and literary idiom, its primary concern is spiritual liberation. The work is known mainly within specialist circles of Vedānta and Sanskrit poetry, and its authorship is sometimes conflated with figures of the same name.

title: Vāmanapurāṇa
author: Vāmanapurāṇa1
classification_notes: The Vāmanapurāṇa is a medieval Sanskrit Purana dedicated primarily to the dwarf avatar Vamana of Vishnu. As part of the eighteen major Puranas, it is an important source for Hindu mythology and religious teachings, widely read by practitioners and scholars of Hinduism.

title: Kāmasūtra
author: Vātsyāyana
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Technical Literature; dcs_subject=Kamashastra; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Vātsyāyana; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Kāmasūtra of Vātsyāyana is the foundational text of the Indian tradition of erotics (Kāmaśāstra), and one of the most internationally known works of Sanskrit literature. It systematically addresses the art of love, pleasure, and household life within a broader framework of dharma, artha, and kāma. While its global recognition is high, in terms of a work's centrality to the entire Sanskrit corpus it is a well-known but still somewhat niche work—widely read but not classed as canonical epic or philosophy.

title: Nyāyabhāṣya
author: Vātsyāyana
classification_notes: Vātsyāyana's Nyāyabhāṣya is the earliest extant commentary on the Nyāyasūtras of Gautama. It established the interpretative framework for the school of logic and epistemology, clarifying arguments on inference, perception, and debate. It is an indispensable text for the study of the Nyāya tradition and has shaped later Indian philosophy.

title: Nyāyabhāṣya
author: Vātsyāyana
classification_notes: Nyāyabhāṣya by Vātsyāyana is the earliest extant commentary on the Nyāya Sūtras of Gautama. It establishes the foundational interpretations of Nyāya epistemology, logic, and metaphysics, and has been central to the Nyāya tradition's development.

title: Nyāyabhāṣya
author: Vātsyāyana
classification_notes: Vātsyāyana's Nyāyabhāṣya is the earliest surviving commentary on the Nyāyasūtras of Gautama, foundational to the Nyāya school of Indian logic and epistemology. Composed in the 4th–5th century CE, it is an indispensable text for the study of Indian philosophy, explicating the sūtras on logic, inference, debate, and metaphysics. It is widely studied in academic contexts.

title: Nyāyabhāṣya
author: Vātsyāyana
classification_notes: Vātsyāyana's Nyāyabhāṣya is the earliest extant commentary on the Nyāya Sūtras of Gautama, and is a foundational text of the Nyāya school of Indian philosophy. It systematically expounds the categories and methods of Nyāya logic and epistemology. This work is of major importance within the history of Indian philosophy and is widely studied by scholars of logic and epistemology. Its length (38354 words) reflects a comprehensive commentary.

title: Nyāyabhāṣya
author: Vātsyāyana
classification_notes: Vātsyāyana's Nyāyabhāṣya is the foundational commentary on Gautama's Nyāya Sūtras. It is an indispensable text for the study of classical Indian logic and epistemology.

title: Advayasiddhi
author: Yoginī Lakṣmīṅkarā
classification_notes: The Advayasiddhi by Yoginī Lakṣmīṅkarā is a short Buddhist tantric treatise on non-dual realization. As a work by a female tantric master, it has particular interest in gender studies and tantric lineages, yet its brevity and specialized esoteric content limit its audience to specialists in Buddhist tantra.

title: Advayasiddhi
author: Yoginī Lakṣmīṅkarā
classification_notes: The Advayasiddhi by Yoginī Lakṣmīṅkarā is a short (546 words) Buddhist tantric philosophical work expounding the doctrine of non-duality (advaya) as the ultimate realization. Lakṣmīṅkarā, a female tantric master, presents a synthesis of Buddhist and Śaiva ideas on the union of wisdom and means. This text is a specialist source in the study of Tantric Buddhism and gender in Indian religious history.

title: Yājñavalkyasmṛti
author: Yājñavalkya
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Dharmashastra; dcs_subject=Dharmashastra; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Yogīśvara Yājñavalkya; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Yājñavalkyasmṛti is a foundational legal and ethical text of Hinduism, attributed to the sage Yājñavalkya. It is a key source in the Dharmashastra tradition.

title: Catuḥślokī
author: Yāmuna
classification_notes: The Catuḥślokī is a brief devotional hymn in four verses by Yāmuna, a key precursor to Rāmānuja's Viśiṣṭādvaita Vedānta. It is a specialist text in the broader Sanskrit corpus and common within the Stotra genre.

title: Catuḥślokī
author: Yāmuna
classification_notes: Yāmuna's Catuḥślokī is a short Vaiṣṇava devotional hymn. It is a significant text in the Śrīvaiṣṇava tradition but has limited circulation beyond that community.

title: Saṃvitsiddhi
author: Yāmuna
classification_notes: Yāmuna’s Saṃvitsiddhi is an important Viśiṣṭādvaita Vedānta treatise arguing for the reality of consciousness as a self-luminous entity. Its 17,426 words constitute a common reference within Vedāntic scholarship, particularly for the Śrīvaiṣṇava tradition.

title: Saṃvitsiddhi
author: Yāmuna
classification_notes: Yāmuna's Saṃvitsiddhi is a Vedāntic work advocating the Viśiṣṭādvaita school, arguing for the reality of consciousness. It is a significant but not central text within that tradition, more specialized than the major Upaniṣads or Brahmasūtra commentaries. Its appeal is largely to scholars of Viśiṣṭādvaita and post-Śaṅkara Vedānta.

title: Ātmasiddhi
author: Yāmuna
classification_notes: The Ātmasiddhi by Yāmuna is a key theological treatise of the Śrīvaiṣṇava tradition, arguing for the existence and nature of the self (ātman) in the framework of Viśiṣṭādvaita Vedānta. It is a well-known work within the Vedānta tradition, often studied as a precursor to Rāmānuja's systematic theology. Its philosophical and devotional depth makes it an important text for scholars of Indian theology and religious philosophy.

title: Ātmasiddhi
author: Yāmuna
classification_notes: Yāmuna's Ātmasiddhi is a key text of the Viśiṣṭādvaita tradition, arguing for the reality of the individual self. Like Yāmuna's Saṃvitsiddhi, it is important within its school but less known outside Viśiṣṭādvaita studies, placing it in the specialist range globally.

title: Nirukta
author: Yāska
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Grammar; dcs_subject=Nirukta; dcs_time_slot=early; dcs_author=Yāska; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Nirukta is a foundational Sanskrit etymological treatise by Yāska, dating from roughly the 5th to 3rd centuries BCE. With 2,938 words, it is the primary text of the vedāṅga of etymology (nirukta), providing etymological explanations of Vedic words as a supplement to the Nighaṇṭu. It is a cornerstone of the Sanskrit grammatical and exegetical tradition, widely studied for its philological method and its insights into Vedic semantics.

title: Paramārthasāra
author: Ādiśeṣa
classification_notes: Paramārthasāra ('Essence of the Highest Truth') is a short Vedantic treatise attributed to Ādiśeṣa, presenting the principles of non-dual philosophy in a compact form. The work belongs to the tradition of prakaraṇa-granthas (introductory manuals) and is sometimes associated with the school of Gauḍapāda.

title: Paramārthasāra
author: Ādiśeṣa
classification_notes: The Paramārthasāra, attributed to Ādiśeṣa, is a short Advaita Vedanta text summarizing the essential teaching of non-duality. It is a specialist work within the Vedanta tradition, studied by philosophers and historians of Indian thought.

title: Anuttaraprakāśapañcāśikā
author: Ādyanātha
classification_notes: Ādyanātha’s Anuttaraprakāśapañcāśikā is a concise exposition of the non-dual philosophy of Kashmir Śaivism, presenting fifty verses on the supreme light (anuttara). It is valued in tantric studies.

title: Anuttaraprakāśapañcāśikā
author: Ādyanātha
classification_notes: The Anuttaraprakāśapañcāśikā by Ādyanātha is a Śaiva Tantric text of fifty stanzas on the supreme light (anuttara-prakāśa). It is a specialist work within the Kashmiri Śaiva tradition.

title: Vallālacarita
author: Ānandabhaṭṭa
classification_notes: Ānandabhaṭṭa's Vallālacarita is a medieval Sanskrit narrative poem (kāvya) recounting the life and deeds of King Vallāla of Bengal. This work (14,159 words) is a significant example of regional historical kāvya. Its subject matter is of interest primarily to scholars of Bengali history and Sanskrit narrative literature, placing it in the specialist tier globally and within the narrative discovery group.

title: Vallālacarita
author: Ānandabhaṭṭa
classification_notes: The Vallālacarita is a biographical kavya by Ānandabhaṭṭa narrating the life of the Sena king Vallālasena. It is a specialist text within Sanskrit poetry, mainly of interest to scholars of medieval Bengali history and kavya.

title: Caryāmelāvaṇapradīpa
author: Āryadeva
classification_notes: Āryadeva's Caryāmelāvaṇapradīpa is an important tantric Buddhist text in the Yoginītantra tradition. It presents advanced sādhana (practice) and is significant within the Vajrayāna canon. Its audience is mainly within Buddhist Tantric studies and is well-regarded by specialists, but it remains within a specialist domain.

title: Caryāmelāvaṇapradīpa
author: Āryadeva
classification_notes: Āryadeva's Caryāmelāvaṇapradīpa is a Buddhist tantric text (ca. 2500 words) attributed to the Mahāsiddha Āryadeva. It belongs to the Caryāgīti tradition and expounds the practice of tantric yoga and ritual. Though concise, it is an important witness to the development of Buddhist tantric literature in Sanskrit, though its readership remains specialist.

title: Jātakamālā
author: Āryaśūra
classification_notes: Āryaśūra's Jātakamālā (Garland of Birth Stories) is a celebrated collection of thirty-four tales depicting the previous lives of the Buddha. Written in elegant Sanskrit prose interspersed with verse, it is a masterpiece of Buddhist narrative literature and a key text for hagiography and early kāvya style. It is widely read in Buddhist studies and holds common-tier global popularity, but is a major work within Buddhist narrative literature.

title: Jātakamālā
author: Āryaśūra
classification_notes: The Jātakamālā by Āryaśūra is a collection of Buddhist jātaka stories (previous lives of the Buddha) in classical Sanskrit prose mixed with verse. Composed around the 4th–5th century CE, it is a celebrated work in Buddhist narrative literature, combining moral instruction with literary artistry. It is frequently studied in the context of Buddhist literature and classical Indian storytelling.

title: Pāramitāsamāsa
author: Āryaśūra
classification_notes: Āryaśūra's Pāramitāsamāsa is a Buddhist text that summarizes the six perfections (pāramitās) of a Bodhisattva, likely in verse form. Āryaśūra is known for his literary works, including the Jātakamālā. This text is of specialist interest within Buddhist studies, particularly for the literature of the perfections and the works of Āryaśūra. Its length (6981 words) suggests a moderate, possibly condensed, treatment.

title: Pāramitāsamāsa
author: Āryaśūra
classification_notes: Āryaśūra's Pāramitāsamāsa is a concise Mahāyāna work summarizing the perfections (pāramitās). It is a significant text in Buddhist literature, attributed to the celebrated author of the Jātakamālā. While not as widely read as Āryaśūra's better-known work, it holds a common place within Buddhist studies, but specialist within the whole Sanskrit corpus.

title: Sāṃkhyakārikā
author: Īśvarakṛṣṇa
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Sanskrit Philosophy; dcs_subject=Samkhya; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Īśvarakṛṣṇa; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: The Sāṃkhyakārikā by Īśvarakṛṣṇa is the foundational text of the Sāṃkhya school of Hindu philosophy, systematizing its dualistic ontology and epistemology. It is a core text in Indian philosophy curricula and scholarly study, widely read and commented upon across traditions.

title: Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā
author: Śatasāhasrikāprajñāpāramitā
classification_notes: The Śatasāhasrikā Prajñāpāramitā is a massive Mahāyāna Buddhist sūtra, the 'Perfection of Wisdom in 100,000 Lines'. It is a foundational text of the Prajñāpāramitā corpus, expounding the doctrine of emptiness (śūnyatā) and the bodhisattva path. As one of the longest Buddhist canonical works, it is a common subject of study in Buddhist studies and Indian philosophy.

title: Aitareyopaniṣadbhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: The Aitareyopaniṣadbhāṣya is a commentary on the Aitareya Upaniṣad by the Advaita philosopher Śaṅkara. It expounds non-dualist interpretations of the text. It is common across the Sanskrit tradition and canonical within Vedantic commentarial literature.

title: Aitareyopaniṣadbhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: Śaṃkara's commentary on the Aitareya Upaniṣad is a key text within the Advaita Vedanta tradition. It provides a non-dualistic interpretation of this early Upaniṣad, linking its ritual and cosmological passages to the unity of Brahman and Ātman. The work is widely consulted by students of Vedanta and Indian philosophy, occupying a common position in classical Sanskrit philosophical literature.

title: Bṛhadāraṇyakopaniṣadbhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: The Bṛhadāraṇyakopaniṣadbhāṣya, attributed to Śaṅkara (Ādi Śaṅkarācārya), is a foundational commentary on the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad, one of the principal Upaniṣads. It is a cornerstone of the Advaita Vedānta tradition and a key text for understanding Śaṅkara's non-dualistic interpretation of scripture. Widely studied in Indian philosophy and religious studies, this commentary commands substantial respect and readership, though its specialized doctrinal content limits its audience compared to core epic works.

title: Bṛhadāraṇyakopaniṣadbhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: Śaṃkara's Bṛhadāraṇyakopaniṣadbhāṣya is a magisterial commentary on the Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad, foundational for Advaita Vedānta. It is widely studied in Indian philosophy and theology, commanding common-to-major status within its scope. Its global readership places it in the common tier, as it is central to its tradition but not universally read across the whole Sanskrit corpus.

title: Chāndogyopaniṣadbhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: Śaṃkara's Chāndogyopaniṣadbhāṣya is an authoritative commentary on the Chāndogya Upaniṣad, a foundational text of the Vedānta tradition. It is widely studied in Advaita Vedānta and Indological scholarship due to its philosophical depth and exegetical rigor. The work commands major status within its commentarial and Vedānta scope, though slightly less prominence across the whole Sanskrit corpus.

title: Chāndogyopaniṣadbhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: Śaṅkara’s Chāndogyopaniṣadbhāṣya is a magisterial commentary on one of the oldest and most important Upaniṣads. It is indispensable for the study of Advaita Vedanta and Śaṅkara’s hermeneutics.

title: Jayamaṅgalā
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: The Jayamaṅgalā, attributed to Śaṃkara, is a commentary on Īśvarakṛṣṇa's Sāṃkhyakārikā. It is an important text in the Sāṃkhya tradition, offering detailed explanations of Sāṃkhya metaphysics.

title: Jayamaṅgalā
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: The Jayamaṅgalā is a commentary on Īśvarakṛṣṇa's Sāṃkhyakārikā, attributed to Śaṅkara (not the Advaita Vedāntin). This 9,305-word text is one of the principal traditional commentaries on the foundational text of the Sāṃkhya school. It provides a systematic and often polemical elucidation of Sāṃkhya metaphysics, cosmology, and epistemology. The work is commonly consulted by scholars of classical Indian philosophy.

title: Kaṭhopaniṣadbhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: Śaṃkara's Kaṭhopaniṣadbhāṣya is a commentary (bhāṣya) on the Kaṭha Upaniṣad by the great Advaita Vedānta philosopher Śaṃkara. This work (11,454 words) is a foundational text for the study of Advaita Vedānta, providing a systematic interpretation of the Upaniṣad's teachings. Śaṃkara's commentaries are widely studied, making this a common text within the philosophy discovery group and the Vedanta scope. Its global popularity is also common, as it is a key text in Indian philosophy curricula.

title: Kaṭhopaniṣadbhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: The Kaṭhopaniṣadbhāṣya by Śaṅkara is the foundational commentary on the Kaṭha Upaniṣad, a central text in Advaita Vedanta. It is a major work within the commentarial tradition, widely studied in philosophy and religious studies for Śaṅkara's interpretation of the Upaniṣadic teachings.

title: Praśnopaniṣadbhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: Śaṅkara's commentary on the Praśna Upaniṣad (Praśnopaniṣadbhāṣya) is a significant work of Advaita Vedanta exegesis. It interprets this important upaniṣad from a strict non-dualist perspective. As part of Śaṅkara's corpus, it is widely read by students of Vedanta and Indian philosophy, achieving a major status within its scope.

title: Praśnopaniṣadbhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: Śaṃkara's commentary (bhāṣya) on the Praśna Upaniṣad is an authoritative Advaita Vedānta exposition of this early Upaniṣad. As a work of Śaṃkara, it holds a major position within the Vedānta commentarial tradition, but within the whole Sanskrit corpus it is a specialist work, consulted primarily by students and scholars of Advaita philosophy. Its modest length (c. 6,900 words) reflects its focused, verse-by-verse commentarial form.

title: Taittirīyopaniṣadbhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: Śaṅkara's commentary on the Taittirīya Upaniṣad is a key text in the Advaita Vedānta tradition. It is widely studied by students of Indian philosophy and is an important exegetical work.

title: Taittirīyopaniṣadbhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: The Taittirīyopaniṣadbhāṣya by Śaṅkara (ca. 16,546 tokens) is a philosophical commentary on the Taittirīya Upaniṣad from the Advaita Vedānta perspective. It is a fundamental text for the study of Śaṅkara's thought and Upaniṣadic exegesis, widely read in Indian philosophy.

title: Īśopaniṣadbhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: Śaṃkara's Īśopaniṣadbhāṣya is a commentary on the Īśā Upaniṣad, one of the principal Upaniṣads. In it, Śaṃkara expounds the non-dual (Advaita) interpretation of the text's teaching on the identity of the individual self with Brahman. As part of the corpus of Śaṃkara's commentaries, it is foundational for Advaita Vedānta and widely studied.

title: Īśopaniṣadbhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: Śaṃkara's commentary on the Īśā Upaniṣad (Īśopaniṣadbhāṣya) is a primary text of the Advaita Vedanta tradition. In this concise work, Śaṃkara interprets the famous Upaniṣad as teaching the identity of the individual self (Ātman) with the Supreme Brahman. It is widely read by students and scholars of Indian philosophy.

title: Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: The Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya (Brahmasūtrabhāṣya) by Śaṃkara is the foundational commentary on Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtras. Its 134,503 words present the system of Advaita Vedānta. The work is a major text in the Sanskrit philosophical corpus and canonical within Vedānta.

title: Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya
author: Śaṃkara
classification_notes: Śaṃkara's Śārīrakamīmāṃsābhāṣya, also known as the Brahmasūtrabhāṣya, is the definitive commentary on Bādarāyaṇa's Brahmasūtras and a foundational text of the Advaita Vedānta tradition. In this work, Śaṃkara systematically expounds non-dualist metaphysics, arguing for the identity of ātman and brahman. It has been a cornerstone of Indian philosophy and theological debate for over a millennium.

title: Śivapurāṇa
author: Śivapurāṇabooks
classification_notes: The Śivapurāṇa is a major Purāṇic text dedicated to Lord Śiva, providing narratives of his deeds, cosmology, mythology, and devotional practices. It is a major work in the wider Sanskrit tradition and canonical within Śaiva Purāṇic literature.

title: Śivapurāṇa
author: Śivapurāṇabooks
classification_notes: Śivapurāṇa is a major Shaiva Purana, comprising mythological narratives, theology, and ritual instructions. It is a foundational text for Śaivism and widely studied in Hindu tradition.

title: Ratnatrayaparīkṣā
author: Śrīkaṇṭha
classification_notes: The Ratnatrayaparīkṣā (Examination of the Three Jewels) is a Jain philosophical work by Śrīkaṇṭha, examining the foundational Jain concepts of right faith, right knowledge, and right conduct. At about 4,600 words, it is a concise treatise aimed at clarifying these essential doctrines. It is a specialist text within Jain literature, valued for its systematic presentation of Jain soteriology.

title: Ratnatrayaparīkṣā
author: Śrīkaṇṭha
classification_notes: Classification: The 'Ratnatrayaparīkṣā' of Śrīkaṇṭha is a medieval Indian philosophical work of the Jain tradition focusing on the 'three jewels' (ratnatraya) of right faith, knowledge, and conduct. It is a specialist and obscure text within the Sanskrit philosophical corpus.

title: Bodhicaryāvatāra
author: Śāntideva
source_metadata_summary: dcs_scope_hint=Buddhist Scripture; dcs_subject=Buddhist; dcs_time_slot=classical; dcs_author=Śāntideva; dcs_completed=false
classification_notes: Śāntideva's Bodhicaryāvatāra ('Introduction to the Practice of Enlightenment') is a classic of Mahāyāna Buddhist literature and practice, composed in Sanskrit verse. The work is a comprehensive guide to the bodhisattva path, emphasizing compassion and wisdom. It is widely studied in Buddhist circles and in academic courses on Indian Buddhism, making it a common text in the global Sanskrit corpus.

title: Sāṃkhyatattvavivecana
author: Ṣimānanda
classification_notes: The Sāṃkhyatattvavivecana by Ṣimānanda is a later philosophical work that expounds the principles of Sāṃkhya dualism. It seeks to clarify the tattvas (categories) of Sāṃkhya, engaging with interpretative issues in the tradition. This text is of specialist interest within Sāṃkhya studies and late classical Sanskrit philosophy.

title: Sāṃkhyatattvavivecana
author: Ṣimānanda
classification_notes: Ṣimānanda's Sāṃkhyatattvavivecana is an independent treatise of the Sāṃkhya darśana, offering an analysis of the fundamental principles (tattvas) of Sāṃkhya metaphysics. Composed in the Early Modern period, the work engages with classical Sāṃkhya-Kārikā exegesis and later Vedantic commentary. While it contributes to the later scholastic development of Sāṃkhya, its narrow circulation and highly technical argumentation render it a specialist text within both philosophy and the broader Sanskrit corpus.
